 Section A - Solicitation/Contract Form  
 
CLAUSES INCORPORATED BY REFERENCE  
 
This draft Request for Proposal (RFP) is being issued solely to solicit feedback from industry. Interested 
parties are encouraged to review the questions  posted to SAM.GOV  and provide responses  by 3 July, along 
with any comments on the draft RFP, to the Contracting Officer, Timothy L. Rivers, at 
timothy.l.rivers@usmc.mil. Please note that this is not a request for proposals  at this time , and no submissions 
will be considered as such. Only the formal solicitation, once released, should be relied upon for the 
Government’s official requirements.  
 A-1  TYPE OF CONTRACT.  This is a single award hybrid Indefinite  Delivery Indefinite Quanty (IDIQ)  contract 
with the flexibility to award task orders on a CPFF, FFP, and Cost Reimbursable (for Materials  and Travel) basis as 
defined by the respective Contract Line Item Number/Sub Line Item Number ( CLINs/SLINs ) against which Task 
Orders will be issued.    
  A-2  ORDERING PERIODS  AND CLIN NUMBERS  
 Base Ordering Period Year One  (CLINs 0001 -0007)  
Base Ordering Period Year Two   (CLINs 1001 -1007)  
Base Ordering Period Year Three             (CLINs 2001- 2007)  
Base Ordering Period Year Four   (CLINs 3001 -3007)  
Base Ordering Period Year Five   (CLINs 4001 -4007)  
Opt I Ordering Period Year Six   (CLINs 5001 -5007)  
Opt I Ordering Period Year Seven             (CLINs 6001 -6007)  
Opt I Ordering Period Year Eight              (CLINs 7001 -7007)  
Opt I Ordering Period Year Nine   (CLINs 8001 -8007)  
Opt I Ordering Period Year Ten   (CLINs 9001 -9007)  
52.217- 8 Ordering Period                          
  
Note:  “X001” is used throughout the SOW where “X” indicates the ordering period 0 through 9 to correlate with 
the ordering periods as identified in paragraph 3 above.

 SECTION C.1 MCPP PROGRAM REQUIREMENT OVERVIEW   
1.A  BACKGROUND  
1.A.1  The Maritime Prepositioning Ships (MPS) program was initiated by the Secretary of 
Defense in August 1979 creating a new option for increasing the responsiveness of Marine Corps operating forces requiring immediate and rapid deployment.  The program requires the prepositioning of equipment and supplies aboard nine Navy owned ships strategically located 
around the world.  Seven of the ships are located in the Indian and Pacific Oceans and two ships 
are located in Jacksonville, FL.  Maritime Prepositioning Force (MPF) is a term utilized to 
describe the combat capability derived when combining a Marine operating f orce and associated 
fly-in echelon (FIE) with the assets loaded aboard the MPSs.  Combined, these ships carry 
equipment and supplies to support approximately 18000 Marines and Sailors for up to 30 days of 
sustained combat.  Equipment and supplies include, but are not limited to; trucks, trailers, 
generators, watercraft, radios, ordnance equipment, tool kits, crew -served weapons, ammunition, 
rations, medical supplies, fuel, and water. Smaller prepositioned sets of equipment and supplies 
(E/S) are maintained in six caves in central Norway as part of the Marine Corps Prepositioning Program – Norway (MCPP -N).  A new Program of Record (POR) consisting of a humanitarian 
assistance/disaster relief (HA/DR) equipment is prepositioned in the Philippines, known as Mari ne Corps Prepositioning Program – Philippines (MCPP -PHIL).  MCPP -PHIL contains 
specific USMC designed equipment and supplies to provide operating forces with equipment required to conduct various training exercises and contingency operations.  Equipment is  
maintained and issued/recovered, at the direction of Headquarters Marine Corps by Blount Island Command (BIC).   Additional prepositioning sites are being considered in Australia and Palau and are undergoing review at HQMC.   Continental United States (CO NUS) based support for 
this entire program is conducted at Blount Island, Jacksonville, Florida. 
1.A.2  The breadth and depth of the E/S prepositioned (over 11,000 national stock numbered 
items totaling slightly over 36 million pieces) provides significant capability and flexibility, 
enabling the combatant commander to conduct numerous crisis response  missions.  The concept 
of combining the speed of strategic airlift with the storage capacity of prepositioned sealift has greatly reduced force closure times in response to contingency scenarios.  Under the MPS concept, the ships will be staged, in advanc e, near the vicinity of a possible crisis area.  If 
deployment is ordered, the ships would move to a rendezvous point to link up with Marine Corps personnel flown to the area by the U. S. Air Force’s Air Mobility Command.  The equipment 
would be quickly downloaded and assigned to combat and support units which then would 
proceed to the objective area.  On 12 March 1983, the Commandant of the Marine Corps (CMC) 
directed the Marine Corps Support Base, Atlantic, hereinafter referred to in its present name, the  
Marine Corps Logistics Command (LOGCOM), to contract for logistics services in support of prepositioned assets. Therefore, a contracted civilian maintenance crew living aboard the ships and at a CONUS industrial facility, would be responsible for performi ng a wide range and depth 
of logistics functions such as preventative and intermediate level corrective maintenance, limited depot level repair (up to 4th echelon depot -level maintenance), equipment modifications, stock 
rotation, calibration, inventory management, management of support systems, supply support, asset visibility and transportation management to include in- transit visibility.  The MPS program 
requires logistics efforts to be accomplished aboard ship, in port areas around the world, and at 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 39 of 425 
 
 an industrial facility located on Marine Corps Support Facility Blount Island (MCSF -BI), 
Jacksonville, Florida during the MPF Maintenance Cycle (MMC).  The Statement of Work 
(SOW) is applicable to both port areas and shipboard activities.  Each ship E/S se t is tailored to 
maximize combat capability and disaster relief options for the combatant commanders.  
1.A.3  In 1981, the United States and the Kingdom of Norway signed a bilateral agreement establishing the Norway Air -Landed Marine Expeditionary Brigade (NALMEB) prepositioning 
program.  The NALMEB program reached initial operating capability (IOC) in January 1990 with the prepositioning of E/S in three equipment caves, three ammunition caves, and two above ground storage hangars.  Shortly thereafter, the collapse of the Soviet Union lessened the military threat to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NA TO) northern flank and the program’s 
relevance in its intended role became less critical.  Subsequently, the program's role and importance to Norway continued to grow and in 1995, Norway assumed a 50% cost sharing arrangement with the Marine Corps.  Throughout the 1990’s the program was used to support NATO out of area missions in support of operations in the Balkans and the first Gulf War.  NALMEB assets were withdrawn beginning in 2003 through 2005 in support of Operation Iraqi Freedom, MPS reconstitution, and to address home station shortfalls for garrison- based Marine 
forces.  In January 2005, the Commandant announced that a prepositioning program would remain in Norway, continue to maintain support to defend Norway, and be capable of deploying globally.  The approved name was changed to the Marine Corps Prepositioning Program – Norway (MCPP -N).  The Secretary of Defense and the Norwegian Minister of Defense signed a 
new Memorandum of Understanding Governing Pre -stockage and Reinforcement of Norway on 
8 June 2005.  MCPP -N serves multiple activities and missions capable of global support to all 
Geographic Combatant Commanders (GCCs).  E/S are stored for selective withdrawal and movement and can be tailored to meet multiple tasks.  Logistics services conduct ed in Norway 
are performed by Norwegian Ministry of Defense (MOD) personnel from the Norwegian Defense Logistics Organization / Marine Expeditionary Brigade (NDLO/MEB) with one exception.  Section C.8 details Marine Corps Maintenance Contractor (MCMC) support for Key Management Infrastructure (KMI) stored in Norway.  The MCMC also provides inventory management, and shipping and receiving services in support of assets being shipped to or returned from Norway.  Maintenance services are provided on the Norway assets stored on or passing through MCSF -BI.  In addition, the MCMC may be tasked to provide specialized 
equipment expertise to and/or assist in increased maintenance or supply requirements in support of the NDLO/MEB personnel. 
1.A.4  In order to meet emerging crisis response and theater engagement requirements 
worldwide, the Marine Corps may explore options to initiate other land- based prepositioning 
programs overseas.   The MCMC may be tasked to provide logistics services in support of these initiatives, such as maintenance, supply/inventory management, and transportation/distribution.  
1.A.5  In 1990, all 13 prepositioning ships existing at the time were downloaded in preparation for the first Gulf War in Saudi Arabia.  The downloads in Southwest Asia left the workforce at Blount Island idle, which resulted substantial reduction of the M CMC CONUS support 
requirements and subsequent Partial Termination for Convenience.  Upon conclusion of the conflict, approximately 45% of the skilled workforce did not return to the program resulting in 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 40 of 425 
 
 several adverse consequences and extended the recovery time of the program by several years.  
In an effort to mitigate that situation from recurring, all subsequent contracts including this procurement contain provisions for the MCMC to provide Other Logis tics Services to the Marine 
Corps.  
1.A.6    In 2022 DON and HQMC made the decision to reduce the number of Full Operating 
Status (FOS) ships from twelve to the current seven ships, with two ships in a Reduced Operating Status (ROS).   As a result, ships in excess of the FOS requirement were  rotated back 
to BICmd where their stocks were downloaded and either utilized for other prepositioning requirements, held for potential future prepositioning requirements, or redistributed to other units or locations.  The term Global Positioning Network ( GPN) was introduced during this period and 
refers to an overarching umbrella of the combination of prepositioning stocks, fleet operating stocks, and the maritime and aerial connectors to connect the nodes.  
1.A.7 In 2024, HQMC directed the establishment of the Marine Corps Prepositioning Program Philippines (MCPP -PHIL).  The new ashore site, located in the Subic Bay Metropolitan 
Authority (SBMA) Freeport zone within the Republic of the Philippines, includes communication radio systems, engineer equipment systems, and light, medium and heavy trucks.  
The MCPP -PHIL PO aims to create operationally relevant capabilities supporting FMF 
campaigning and provide support to Commander, INDOPACIFIC theater campaign objec tives. 
MCPP -PHIL PO establishment consisted of a phased and incremental approach that aligned 
service- wide actions with resources and host nation authorizations.  A portion of the PO supports 
humanitarian assistance/disaster relief (HA/DR) as an equipment set with associated sustainment was shipped to Subic Bay Philippines after the coordinating all necessary host nation agreements in December of 2024.  The equipment arrived at SBMA in February of 2025, and the first PO equipment withdrawal occurred in Marc h 2025.  
1.A.8  The primary effort of all work shall be accomplished at the Marine Corps Support Facility Blount Island (MCSF -BI), Jacksonville, Florida utilizing Government provided facilities,

hand tools (reference C ollective Bargining Agreement (C BA) Contract  attachment ( 10)  The 
MMC facilities are Government Furnished Facilities (GFF) and are located at MCSF -BI, 
Jacksonville, Florida.  Facility maintenance of the acreage and buil dings is the responsibility of 
the MCSF -BI which has contracted through the Navy Region Southeast service provider for 
roads, grounds, and building support.  The Commanding Officer (CO), Blount Island Command (BICmd) is also the CO, MCSF -BI.  The MCMC perf orms Government Furnished Equipment

to all organizations on the Installation, e.g., wash rack and fueling, and will be required to share,

1.B  OVERALL MCPP SCOPE .  The MCMC shall provide a full range of logistics 
services in support of the three Marine Corps’ Prepositioning Program efforts: the MPS Program, MCPP -N and MCPP- PHIL .  The number of ships and land based sites may increase or decrease 
based on Marine Corps operational needs. The MCMC shall provide all management, supervision, administrative/clerical equipment, supplies, consumables, and personal protective equipment required for all personnel performing under this effort (except GFP); cost associated with administrative/clerical equipment, supplies, consumables, and personal protective equipment shall be included in CLIN X001.  This SOW delineates work to be performed by the MCMC for maintenance of equipment and materiel management such that required assets are attained, preserved, packaged, identified accurately in the Business Operating Systems/Accountable Property Systems of Record identified in paragraph 2.C.1, and available in support of the Marine Corps Prepositioning Programs (MCPP) and deployed forces world-wide.  
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 42 of 425 
 
 In addition to prepositioning support, the MCMC shall provide operational logistics services to 
engaged Marine Corps and Department of Defense (DoD) operating forces.  These services shall enable the operating forces to rapidly and successfully conduct and quickly recover from assigned missions across the full spectrum of expeditionary warfare.  SOW Attachment C.1 -3 
(MMC Notional Plan of Action & Milestones (POA&M) defines the phasing of MMC operations conducted separately and jointly by the MCMC and Government for both Marine Corps and Navy equipment.  Tasks assigned to the MCMC are not limited to materiel management and maintenance as there are specific support activities necessary for sustainment of this program (e.g. wash rack operations, fuel station, etc. as described in this SOW).  This SOW also defines some tasks performed by the Government.  Government tasks described are provided to ensure a clear understanding of the total program and to help define the division of responsibilities and necessary interfaces between the Government, other contractors and the MCMC in this unique program.  BICmd has been designated as Executive Agent for the MCPP by the Marine Corps and as such shall be the organization, which shall provide all guidance, direction, oversight, and liaison required for the prepositioning programs.  The maintenance level to be accomplished at the MMC facility or to equipment prepositioned aboard ship is dependent upon availability of special tools, proximity of established facilities (Marine  Corps/Navy 
maintenance facilities), and the availability of funds.   Further scope specifics are provided as follows:  
1.B.1  MMC .  The MPS rotations are scheduled to meet American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) 
requirements for hull inspections and ship certifications.  As such, ship schedules and MMC time frames are generally available several years in advance and are tied to dry dock p eriods (see  
SOW  Attachments C.1 -8  MMC -14 schedule and proposed MMC-15 schedule, and C.1-9 Navy 
Watercraft Plan.  The MCMC work effort is extremely time sensitive for several reasons.  First, the MMC effort shall be accomplished during the ship's dry dock period and secondly, when the ship is out of its area of responsibility (AOR) the squadron is in a degraded status, i.e., one seventh of the squadron's capability is not available to the regional GCC.  Determination of logistics requirements is a n ongoing process which shall be conducted in accordance with this 
document.  MCMC shall provide logistics services in support of the program directives found throughout this SOW.  MCMC shall, primarily in government owned CONUS- based facilities, 
perform e quipment maintenance and materiel management operations required to accomplish up 
to and including intermediate level maintenance of ground combat support equipment. 
 
1.B.2  Program Sustainment.  MCMC shall provide logistics services in support of the program 
directives found throughout this SOW.  MCMC shall perform primarily in government owned CONUS based facilities, those materiel management operations required to sustain MPS, MCPP -
N and MCPP-PHIL operations.  Toward this end, these materiel management operations are described throughout the SOW and include; but are not limited to quality control, organic supply, and environmental and safety compliance.  The MCMC shal l plan for materiel management of 
government assets in a Care of Supplies in Storage (COSIS) status against the current ship’s/MCPP -PHIL Production Cycle/embarkation period of performance.  COSIS services 
include preventive and corrective maintenance, application of equipment modifications, and equipment preservation and corrective maintenance.  
 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 43 of 425 
 
 1.B.3  Shipboard Operations.  Shipboard work performance required by this SOW shall be 
accomplished aboard deployed MPS and Layberthed ROS ship(s).  In addition, maintenance may 
be required ashore at the off-load site during exercises and contingencies .  Shipboard logistics require ments are specified in Section C.7.  The MCMC shall place necessary personnel aboard 
each FOS and ROS ship to perform COSIS on the embarked equipment and materiel. 
 
1.B.4  Navy Support Requirements.  The Navy effort includes the Navy Support Element (NSE) 
and Naval Construction Element (NCE) equipment outlined in their respective Tables of Allowance (TOA) assigned to support the MPF Program, including rotational spares and Navy 
organic equipment.  The MCMC provides the maintenance, repair and logistical services for the Navy mission as detailed in the SOW.  The MCMC’s Navy support organization shall provide optimal equipment readiness while meeting limitations associated with cost and funding, production requirements and schedule timelines.  The MCMC shall deliver Navy products and services in accordance with standards identified in Section C.6 of the SOW and all applicable Navy and Marine Corps orders and directives. 
 
 
1.B.5  OCONUS Operations 
 
1.B.5.a  MCPP -N.  The USMC has prepositioned equipment in Norway for MCPP- N that 
contains all classes of supply to include communications security materiel/controlled cryptographic items (COMSEC/CCI) used to provide cryptographic security of US Department of the Navy com munications.  Due to the sensitivity of COMSEC/CCI, this 
equipment requires U. S. personnel to account and maintain the equipment while it is prepositioned in Norway.  BICmd has established a MCPP- N KMI Local Element (LE) 
subaccount that the MCMC shall maintain.  This requires a two -person integrity rule to 
be maintained during normal and extended working hours.  The U. S. MCMC personnel shall be responsible for all COMSEC materiel held by the LE account to include the following: 
 
• Administration and management, security, accountability and daily operations of the LE account to include the conduct of inventories 
• Storage/handling/disposition: safe storage and positive control of all COMSEC materiel and conduct inspections to determine that proper physical security and control are provided 
• Transportation: proper physical security measures during transport of COMSEC materiel 
• Maintenance of all communication/electronic equipment  
• Establish, test, and maintain Emergency Action Plan (EAP) for COMSEC materiel  
• Maintain all records and files in Norway, required by EKMS-1, using computer hardware 
and software supplied by the Naval Communications Security Materiel System (NCMS)  
 1.B.5.b  MCPP-PHIL.  The MCMC shall perform the functions necessary to issue assets at the equipment location, recover assets, ensure asset serviceability, and coordinate maintenance actions for assets requiring maintenance above the capability of the contractor for the MCPP -PHIL Equipment Set.  The Contractor shall facilitate MCPP -
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 44 of 425 
 
 PHIL management and operations, to include maintenance management, maintenance, 
supply chain management, and KMI local element responsibilities in accordance with Appendix "K MPF SOW, container management, and associated reporting requirements. The Contractor shall execute distribution functions in support of MCPP- PHIL.  The 
MCMC shall provide inventory management which includes receiving, receipting, accounting for, storing, maintaining, issuing, and recovering equipment and supplies, as well as Preventive Maintenance Checks, and Services (PMCS), Care of Supplies in Stores (COSIS), and Corrective Maintenance (CM) within the field -level echelon of 
maintenance.  The MCMC shall also provide commercial shipping and receiving services to include; coordination with SBMA/host nation officials, customs processing, port handling, stevedoring and related terminal services, drayage, hustling, and transportation of assets to U. S. government leased warehouses to support MCPP- PHIL assets being 
shipped to or returned from the Philippines. Maintenance services are provided on the MCPP -PHIL assets stored on or passing through MCSF-BI.  In addition, the MCMC may 
be tasked to provide specialized equipment expertise to and/or assist in increased maintenance or supply requirements in support of the operating forces during exercise, HA/DR, or contingency withdrawals.   In accordance with DODI 3020.37 section 6, all services provided in support of MCPP-PHIL are considered mission essential and shall be performed by the MCMC in the event of crises, at various locations within the Republic of the Philippines, or other locations in the USINDOPACOM AOR as required.  MCMC unique MCPP- PHIL requirements  are specifically called out in respective subsections of 
this SOW.  The MCPP- PHIL work  hour requirement shall be in accordance with Host 
Nation Labor Laws Monday - Friday.  Additional hours or days may be authorized by the Government as mission requirements dictate. The Contractor shall not work, to include processing or reprocessing employees, on Host Nation Holidays unless authorized by the Government.  The Contractor shall, as needed, support unplanned events such as the augmentation of other Marine Corps theater initiatives, identification of other Marine Force maintenance deficiencies, and any other emerging tasks incidental to expansion of Marine Corps theater operations. These will be negotiated on a case-by- case basis. The 
Contractor shall report any situation of interest that affects MCPP -PHIL equipment 
visibility, accountability, an d security to the Government. 
 
1.B.6  RESERVED  
 
 1.B.7  Military Contingency Support Worldwide 
 
1.B.7.a  Deployed.  During the period that equipment is downloaded in support of a 
contingency, equipment is no longer available to rotate through the maintenance cycle.  The MCMC, if requested, shall be required to provide in- theater operational level 
logistics services to free up the operating forces to focus on contingency operations.  Operational logistics support could include such services as Military Equipment (ME) rotation, sustainment support, retrograde operations, management, and maintenance of equipment pools, providing field service representatives, management of reparable issue points, preservation/packaging and packing services, equipment maintenance, supply 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 45 of 425 
 
 chain services, and program management.  As an essential part of national mobilization, 
there is a requirement for the MCMC to be prepared to divide the current workforce (or provide immediate supplementation of current workforce) and deploy to an off- load 
site/arrival and assembly area (AAA) in support of a potential contingency, or to a mature theater to provide operational logistics support to deployed Marines, and/or to a MPS regeneration site to refurbish and reload MPS after a contingency.  The MCMC s hall 
sustain operations during periods of extended deployment.    Planning cannot be specific as contingency requirements are generally not known until just before execution, however, there shall be a requirement for maintenance personnel, supply expertise, container packing supervision, and container repair at to be determined Outside CONUS (OCONUS) site(s) to support deploying units participating in contingency operations.  
1.B.7.b  Continental United States (CONUS) Based Support .  With Marine forces 
engaged in contingencies, BICmd may be tasked to perform similar services such as maintenance on Remain Behind Equipment (RBE) at CONUS installations (e.g. Camp Lejeune, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA) and provide overflow maintenance support to Marine Corps and other service depots.  Other unforeseen circumstances such as ship schedule changes or the transition from current legacy ships to future ships may result in reduced logistics requirements at the Blount Island facility.  During these periods, the Marine Corps Program Management team may bring in additional Marine Corps or DoD overflow maintenance from depots or intermediate maintenance activities to maintain work force skills and to avoid layoffs of the program's skilled workforce. 
 
1.B.8  Exercise Support.  The MCMC shall provide Exercise Support as indicated in respective 
Exercise Support Letters.  Exercise Support includes the deployment of personnel and BICmd resources in direct support of Marine Corps/Navy Operating Forces utilizing MCPP assets.  For MPS exercises, the MCMC shall source shipboard personnel to meet the requirements identified in the respective Exercise Support Letter.  For MCPP -N exercises, personnel support identified 
in the Exercise Support Letter, i.e., ordnance mechanics, may be resourced from MCMC assigned to MCSF Blount Island. For MCPP- PHIL exercises, MCMC personnel will be 
primarily sourced from employees permanently assigned to the MCMC’s MCPP- PHIL team and 
be externally augmented (e.g. MCMC at MCSF-BI) as outlined in the Exercise Support L etter.  
The duration of exercise support varies from weeks to months dependent upon the concept of operations.  Exercise support includes Overtime, Materials (inclusive of subcontractor support), Other Direct Costs, and Travel; Materials and Other Direct Costs shall be allocated to CLIN X006, Travel shall be allocated to CLIN X005.  Labor augmentation from the MCMC assigned to MCSF Blount Island must be approved by the Government.

1.D  PROGRAM COMPLEXITY 
 
1.D.1  Program Interdependencies.  The MCPP operates in a multifaceted environment 
characterized by levels of detail and complexity, which necessitate enterprise- wide integration 
and requires that the MCMC shall maintain an appropriate span of control over  operational and 
programmatic complexities to mitigate operational and program management risks.  Paragraph 1.D is provided as historical background.  
 1.D.2  Detail Complexity 
 1.D.2.a  Per the government -approved strategic plan, developed by the separate Plans & 
Data Support (P&DS) contract, MPSs rotate one at a time, approximately every 42 months, through BICmd aboard the Marine Corps Support Facility Blount Island (MCSF -
BI) in  Jacksonville, Florida to go through its respective, time -constrained MPF 
Maintenance Cycle (MMC) lasting approximately 6 months depending on the ship type, and encompassing all three segments of the MCPP.  Occasionally there are gap periods between ships.  During those periods, direction is provided by the government on priority of work effort for shore -based sites and other activities (WIR, COSIS, etc.).  Once the 
ship is offloaded via the Stevedoring & Related Terminal Services (S&RTS) contract, it is here that the MCMC performs the full range of sustainment, refurbishment, 
replenishment and specialized preservation logistics services that ensure the combat 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 47 of 425 
 
 readiness of equipment and supplies for subsequent years of forward deployed long- term 
storage.  
 
1.D.2.b  During this period (time durations are dependent upon ship type) the MCMC is required to thoroughly refurbish all Marine Corps and Navy ground tactical military equipment by performing preventive and corrective maintenance, applying modifications, ensuring the recalibration of Test Measurement and Diagnostic Equipment (TMDE), inventorying/replacing collateral equipment, and exchanging obsolete equipment with newly fielded assets. The MCMC’s responsibility is to ensure that the materiel a Marine warfighter requires to operate independently in a completely expeditionary environment 
for 30 days of sustained combat is attained, maintained, and in a ready -to-issue status, to 
include accurate data in the respective systems of record, prior to re -embarkati on aboard 
the respective MPS or shipment to one of the ashore prepositioning programs.   1.D.2.c  Each ship contains an average of 700 Principal End Items (PEIs) (ranging from 447 to 871, depending on ship type) square loaded with their SL -3 (ancillary equipment) 
as well as associated assets such as communications and electronics equipment; or dnance 
equipment (weapons systems, optics equipment, etc ).  The specialized logistics services 
performed under this program include inspection, servicing, corrosion control, corrective maintenance (as required), armoring, application of any required modifi cations, 
association of collateral equipment and mobile loaded cargo, integration of communications/ electronics equipment, application of Total Asset Visibility (TAV) Radio Frequency Identification Tags (RFID), preservation for long- term storage and 
docum entation of all of these actions in the respective APSR.  
 
1.D.2.d  Additionally, each ship averages 436 shipping containers (ranging from 291 to 604, depending on ship type) loaded with over 16,000 line items of sustainment supplies to include rations; packaged Petroleum, Oils, and Lubricants (POL); barrier/forti fication 
materiel; medical equipment/supplies, and spare repair parts/sub -assemblies. The MCMC 
performs supply and transportation support, such as inventory management, stock rotation (for rations, shelf -life expiry items), calibration, corrosion control / prevention, 
total asset visibility marking/tracking, and transportation management. A single ship’s MMC production requirements generate approximately 11,000 supply requisitions.  
 1.D.2.e  Finally, once the equipment and supplies have been back -loaded aboard each 
MPS via the separate S&RTS contract, the MCMC maintains custody, provides COSIS for this materiel while forward deployed  and prepares/issues it to Marine commanders for MP F exercises, and campaigns to transition to conflict. The MCMC also maintains a 
small subset of the equipment and supplies outside CONUS that are strategically prepositioned in Norwegian storage caves in support of MCPP -N (sensitive 
communications equipment and crew served weapons that the Host Nation military is not allowed to maintain for the USMC) and Humanitarian and Disaster Relief (HADR) assets in secured storage locations in the Philippines.  
 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 48 of 425 
 
 1.D.2.f  The Program description above in paragraph 1.D.2 illustrates a prescriptive and 
interdependent process involving many variables. The volume of materiel processed over such a short period of time is a balancing act of program and project management.   
1.D.3  Scope of  Complexity 
 1.D.3.a  The very nature of the strategic MCPP is centered on responding to the unknown with a credible capability in short- to-no-notice time frames. Therefore, BICmd’s 
operational mandate is to: 1) attain 100% of the Commandant of the Marine Corps’ stated objective for equipment and supplies; 2) configure and maintain all of the prepositioned materiel to 100% combat readiness; 3) achieve 100% data accuracy of equipment and supplies in the respective APSR; and 4) maximize the forward deployed “on station” t ime 
in support of the GCC’s war plan requirements. Countless stakeholders directly and indirectly influence the day -to-day pre-planned and programmed MMC activities, 
introducing significant complexities directing operational mission and/or program management changes that create program -wide resource loading ripple effects. Adequacy 
of resources shall be assessed and adjusted by the Program Management team and the MCMC.  
 1.D.3.b  This complexity routinely falls completely outside of BICmd’s ability to forecast or influence. Some examples and impacts to on-going MMC production operations follow:  Combat and HA/DR Contingency Operations: Crisis response missions require the task organization and deployment of Technical Assistance & Advisory Teams (TAAT ) which draw government and MCMC personnel away from the Blount Island MMC to prepare and issue prepositioned materiel to Marine and Navy operating forces. Subsequent post-contingency reconstitution efforts are based on the scope of sustainment items consumed and the damage to combat equipment that must be reset to 100% readiness for the next contingency. Historically, reconstitution has been executed in theater (post DESERT STORM when half of BICmd and the MCMC deployed forward) or at Blount Island (nine ships worth of equipment retrograded to BICmd post IRAQI FREEDOM I).  Exercises: The past 25 years have witnessed the quadrupled usage of prepositioned materiel for Marine Corps operating force training, engagement opportunities with foreign countries and their militaries, and deterrence operations. The number and scope of these activities are generally only determined by the GCCs and Marine Corps a year 
ahead of time; therefore, the MCPP contract must be flexible enough to meet this demand signal while concurrently executing MMC and shore prepositioning operations. Post-exercise corrective maintenance (CM) opportunities are typically deferred to the next 
scheduled MMC opportunity due to time, space, resource and schedule constraints.  Therefore, the actual CM requirements are unknown until the ship is offloaded at BICmd and the equipment is fully assessed, further impacting the MMC.  
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 49 of 425 
 
 New Doctrine and Employment Concepts: USMC efforts to develop doctrine to counter 
and destroy a constantly evolving enemy threat results in perpetual “reshaping” of the prepositioned combat capability sets produced by the MCPP.  Changes in doctrine often occur faster than they can be implemented due to the 42- month maintenance cycle 
process to access the prepositioned materiel. This process is further complicated by the necessity to “roll -down” assets from one ship to another to place it on the correct 
platform to meet operating force requirements.  HQMC is assessing other possible ashore 
prepositioning programs in Australia and Palau and continues to refine ship schedules and afloat capabilities. The Marine Corps may also utilize MPSs as platforms to suppor t 
small Marine units for engagement operations.  This also requires TAAT deployment impacting the MMC/MCPP contract.   Combat Equipment Modernization: The MCPP includes tens of thousands of line items of combat materiel spanning virtually the entire Marine Corps’ equipment inventory. Equipment modernization occurs along enterprise programmatic timelines with varying speed,  prioritization, and resourcing. Modernization is constant and fielding for larger 
scale weapons systems is generally forecast early enough to be included in deliberate MMC plans.  Urgent equipment modifications, modernizations or procurements in response to an operating force Urgent Needs Statement infrequently arrives with little prior notice during an ongoing MMC Production period and must then be integrated into the equipment sets as rapidly as possible. This often requires New Equipment Training (NET) to be provided to the MCMC’s workforce by the Government and induction into an ongoing MMC Production period, directly impacting current operations.  Ship Platform Modernization: Military Sealift Command (MSC) modernization programs 
(e.g. the transition from legacy prepositioning ships to LMSRs; MPF ships reaching end of service life; etc.) have resulted in significant changes to the MMC production activities to configure equipment for long term storage (e.g. mobile loads, truck cabs up or down, etc.). The introduction of two T -AKE sustainment ships to the prepositioning fleet 
necessitated a complete MMC production methodology change requiring palletized loads for those specific platforms vice using Twenty Foot E quivalent Unit (TEU) ISO shipping 
containers for the prepositioned sustainment assets on all other Roll -On/Roll -Off 
(RO/RO) MPSs.  
 Ship Availability: Periodically MPSs fail to return to BICmd for backload as scheduled per the MMC calendar due to extended shipyard availability periods that are caused by ship alterations/modifications driven by operational capability needs, or to addres s 
maintenance issues. The typical impact to the MCPP is an unprogrammed, additive COSIS requirement for that ship’s capability set of prepositioned materiel by the MCMC until the outgoing ship can be scheduled for backload. These COSIS efforts impact MMC production for the next ship in the cycle by drawing on resources to meet this unprogrammed requirement.  Additionally, OPNAV and HQMC have at times changed the number and types of ships available to the program.  
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 50 of 425 
 
 Weather: MCSF -BI encounters occasional destructive/severe weather events resulting in 
lost production days.  Production hours lost during these days must be made up to meet 
ship backload and GCC on-station requirements.   1.D.3.c  A review of the most recent MMCs reveal numerous major schedule changes that resulted in significant second/third order effects to the MCPP. These major program changes are heightened by hundreds of individual changes to equipment and supplies being processed during each ship’s MMC. The impact of changes to the MCPP result in 
an inherent complexity that must be mitigated. The MCPP enterprise must be flexible enough to adjust to these imposed changes.   
1.E  PROGRAM OPERATIONAL GOALS  
 
1.E.1  Effectively Meet Program Operational Goals .  The primary goal of the MCPP is to 
effectively perform all SOW actions necessary to ensure the expectations of our external customers, the Fleet Marine Forces (FMF), are met.  Full attainment of the Prepositioning Objective (PO), combat readiness of the prepositioned materiel, and accurate/reliable data visibility is the end -state of this effort.  The PO is published in the NAVMC 2907 and it 
establishes the approved inventory list for MPF and ashore programs. 
 
1.E.2  Efficiently Meet Program Operational Goals .   The second goal of the MCPP is to ensure 
the Marine Force (MARFOR)/Marine Expeditionary Force (MEF) requirements are met in the most cost-efficient manner possible while remaining in conformance with this SOW.  MCMC shall use the Government provided Enterprise QMSS (currently MCPIC iPrime Inspection Web and eQMS modules) to assist with external and internal tracking, trending, and tracing of performance.  Upon identification of unsatisfactory trends, the MCMC shall correct/report the causes of nonconformance to Contracting Officer (KO), Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR), and Contracting Officer’s Technical Representatives (COTRs) through the QMSS as directed in Section C.2.  NOTE:  The Government will also use the QMSS to evaluate the quality of the MCMC products and services. 
  
1.F ORGANIZATIONAL RESPONSIBILITY .  The MCMC shall be directly 
responsible for execution of the contract and SOW requirements.  MCMC, when exercising the requirements of this SOW and communicating with other DoD organization(s) on behalf of the BICmd, shall notify the Government when external Government organizations are not responsive thereby causing delays that may impact meeting contract requirements.  The nature of the MCPP effort as described above calls for close daily communication and coordination between the MCMC and Government.  Figure 1 depicts the organizational lines of communication necessary for Program success.  Although informal communication takes place on a daily basis between the two contracted parties, directive authority rests with the KO IAW the contract and the Federal Acquisition Regulations (FAR).

1.H.2  MCMC Personnel, Disciplines, and Specialties.  The MCMC shall accomplish the 
assigned work by employing and utilizing qualified and skilled personnel with the minimum combinations of education, training, certification and experience as identified below and in SOW Attachment C.1 -7 Employee Qualifications.   
 
• MCMC shall provide an on- site Program Manager (PM) with a minimum of 15 years of 
experience with demonstrated responsibility for overall management and coordination of relevant logistics management services.   
 
• Subordinate Managers shall have a minimum of 10 years’ experience in the respective areas of responsibility and four years of Supervisory experience.    
• Supervisors shall have a minimum of six years’ experience in their respective areas of responsibility.      
• All personnel requiring special certifications (i.e. PMP, IT Certifications, etc.) shall have 
maintained such certifications for the most recent three years.   
 
• The MCMC Quality Manager shall possess or must obtain the ASQ certification “Manager of Quality/Organizational Excellence Certification CMQ/OE” within two years of contract award and have requisite seven (7) years’ experience in the quality profession.   
• All MCMC auditors shall be certified Lead Auditors to the current version of ISO 19011 and ISO 9001 respectively.  Certifications shall be updated within one year of a new version being published.   
• DAWIA and FAC certifications may be submitted as an alternative to PMI and ASQ certifications.    1.H.2.a  The MCMC shall provide sufficient personnel to perform the various tasks associated with the MCPP.  Those personnel shall be fully trained  in their specialty skills 
M6700425R0007

 in accordance with applicable Marine Corps Orders and directives, the NAVMC 1200.1_ 
(Military Occupational Specialties Manual), Department of Defense guidance and directives, Federal, State and Local laws and regulations, industry standards, and other technical documentation.   
 1.H.2.b  The MCMC shall meet with Command Senior Leadership and principal staff on a semi -annual basis as part of the Command’s ISO 9001 Joint Management Review and 
present the MCMC’s Program Personnel Support Plan (PPSP) (CDRL 1030) for the following year for government review and concurrence.  The MCMC’s PPSP shall include an expected requirement to capacity analysis to ensure the MCMC has adequate personnel resources to perform the tasks associated with the MCPP.  The plan shall include an Organizational Chart and Personnel Roster of all MCMC personnel.  The 
Organizational Chart and Personnel Roster must differentiate individuals dedicated to supporting each of the MCPP efforts and those supporting Navy Operations.  The Personnel Roster must include titles and assigned numbers (if applicable).  Vacant hourly billets shall be filled within 60 calendar days with qualified personnel.  Key personnel vacancies are addressed in paragraph 1.H.2.e.  
 1.H.2.c  The MCMC shall provide a monthly staffing report (CDRL 1031) by the last day of each month.  The staffing report shall include the billet titles, planned number of personnel from the PPSP, vacancy(ies) quantities for those billets, duration of those billet vacancies, and hiring activity comments for those vacancies . 
 1.H.2.d  MCMC is responsible for ensuring all personnel are trained to perform required duties as stated within the SOW and maintain such training requirements with minimal impact to production schedules in accordance with applicable Command Orders and Technical Manuals.  The MCMC shall maintain training documentation for all employees 
and shall make training records/certifications available to the government upon request.  Training for all MCMC personnel shall not be a direct charge under this contract; the MCMC shall internally manage/coordinate and process all training and associated travel to meet the requirements of this contract.  Additionally, MCMC employees shall complete government required training on an annual basis as outlined in SOW Attachment C.1 -6.

1.H.3  Security Considerations and Class ification . This contract requires the MCMC to possess a 
Secret Facility Security Clearance in accordance with Contract Attachment # 4 (DD Form 254) 
and will require certain contracted personnel to gain and maintain a favorably adjudicated T1 or T3 investigation IAW MCO 5510.18B. All security classification requirements shall be fully identified, reviewed, and validated in accordance with Contract Attachment #4 (DD Form 254) prior to award of the IDIQ contract. Compliance with the DD Form 254 is mandatory to ensure all security requirements are met for the Marine Corps Prepositioning Program. The MCMC and all sub -contractors (through the MCMC ) shall adhere to all aspects of DoD Directive 5220.22-M, 
National Industrial Security Program Operating Manual (NISPOM). All personnel identified to perform on this contract shall maintain compliance with Department of Defense, Department of the Navy, and Marine Corps Information and Personnel Security Policy to include open background investigations (as required) prior to performance IAW NISPOM. By nature of this program, the MCMC requires overseas access in Norway, Diego Garcia, Saipan, the Philippines and many other locations where exercises and contingency operations may occur.

 1.H.3.a  The following billets require a favorably adjudicated T3 investigation:  Manager 
and Supervisory personnel, information technology (IT) personnel, arms/ ammunition/ explosives (AA&E) personnel, and ordnance maintenance personnel (small arms/optics ). 
Additionally, personnel not listed above but that will operate in secure spaces, work with National Security Information (NSI), or work on IT -II level systems shall also possess a 
favorable T3 investigation. Responsibility for submission of investigations to gain eligibility is per the current edition of MCO 5510.18B.    1.H.3.b  Common Access Card (CAC) Requirement.  All personnel shall meet eligibility criteria to be issued a CAC.  In order to meet the eligibility criteria, MCMC employees requiring a CAC, must obtain and maintain a favorably adjudicated Personnel Securit y 
Investigation (PSI).   Prior to authorizing a CAC, the employee’s Defense Information System for Security (DISS) record must indicate a completed and favorably adjudicated PSI or (at a minimum) that a PSI is submitted and accepted (opened).   The minimum acceptable investigation is a T1.  If the MCMC employee’s open investigation closes and is not favorably adjudicated, the MCMC shall immediately retrieve the employee’s CAC.  The Facility Security Officer (FSO) is responsible for notifying the BICmd Secur ity 
Manager if any individual performing on this contract receives an unfavorable adjudication after being issued a CAC.  All MCMC personnel issued CACs, and those performing IT -II functions, will be “Owned” in DISS by the employing company.  The  
MCMC sha ll notify the Government (written notice) within twenty -four hours of any 
MCMC personnel added or removed from the contract that have been granted classified access, issued a Common Access Card (CAC) and/or Defense Biometric Identification System (DBIDS) b adge. Ensure any individual requesting a replacement CAC, without 
turning in the old CAC, provides a written statement attesting to the circumstances 
surrounding the request for a new ID Card.  Ensure CACs are turned into the Trusted Agent Security Manager  (TASM) or designated Trusted Agent (TA) upon termination of 
an employee or upon termination of this contract.  Report to the Contracting Officer in writing any misuse/abuse/ fraudulent use of Identification cards which may provide grounds for immediate re vocation of the ID Card.  
 1.H.3.c  The MCMC shall immediately remove any employee that endangers national security.  Report violations of security requirements to the BICmd Security Office to allow dual reporting to the appropriate consolidated adjudication facility (CAF). The FSO must notify the BICmd Security Manager of any adverse/derogatory information associated with the 13 Adjudicative Guidelines/Factors concerning any MCMC personnel issued a CAC, regardless of whether a DISS Incident Report is submitted.    1.H.3.d  Prior to employment, MCMC employees shall coordinate with the BICmd Security Office for submission of electronic fingerprints.  Upon completion of fingerprinting, the BICmd Security Office will provide the SF -85 or SF -86 application.  
The MCMC sha ll implement staffing policies and procedures to ensure that pre -
employment requirements are met in a timely manner to provide sufficient manning upon contract implementation and to meet workload fluctuations thereafter.

1.I  EQUIPMENT AND MATERIEL ACCOUNTABILITY  
 
1.I.1  By virtue of being the MCMC PM for this contract, the MCMC shall maintain strict materiel accountability and custodial responsibility of all Government -owned equipment and 
supplies within the program.  The scope of operations in support of the MCPP contract requires MCMC management of various retail level supply accounts, as follows:  intermediate level accounts, to include a General Account (GA), a Maintenance  Float/Reparable Issue Point (RIP) 
and associated sub -floats; multiple organic/consumer level accounts and sub- custody accounts 
associated with MPSRON(s), MCPP -PHIL and MCPP -N prepositioned E/S; and on- island 
accounts associated with General Equipment (Gar rison Mobile Equipment (GME), Garrison 
Property and non- tactical Non -Standard Equipment) and Government Furnished Equipment 
(GFE)).  
 1.I.2  The MCMC is to execute supply chain and inventory management in accordance with standards defined in Section C.4, which are governed by Marine Corps orders, manuals, instructions, and technical directives, as well as Department of Defense, Department of the Navy, federal, state, and local laws and regulations, industry standards, and other technical documentation, except where modified by this contract to meet unique program requirements.  While the MCMC will execute sub -custody responsibilities and property record adjustments in 
accordance with MCO P4400.151 (intermediate level inventory), MCO 4400.201, and UM 4000-125, the approval authority thresholds identified by personnel assignment/billet, associated with adjustments to property records, rests solely with Government.  Therefore, all requests for adjustments to property records, regardless of dollar value or transaction type, shall be forwarded by the MCMC to the Government’s Accountable Officer (AO), via voucher requests, prior to making adjustm ents to accountable property records.  All resulting Money Value Gain Loss 
(MVGL) notices shall be forwarded to the Government for signature.  Likewise, formal appointment of Responsible Officers (RO) shall be completed by the Government AO, upon 
receipt o f RO nomination letters provided by the MCMC/PM.  
 1.I.3   Shipboard.  Tools, test equipment, office spaces, and equipment furnished as part of the 
ship's charter agreement shall be available for use by the MCMC but shall remain as part of ship's assets.  MCMC shall conform to ship’s regulations in the use  of these assets.  
 1.I.4 Embarked.  MCMC shall receipt for all embarked prepositioned assets and shall, after 
accepting assets, be responsible for those assets until relieved by the KO or as specifically outlined by this SOW.

1.J  GOVERNMENT FURNISHED PROPERTY (GFP) AND SERVICES  
 
1.J.1  The MCMC shall maintain all Government Furnished Property (GFP) in a satisfactory, clean and usable condition; GFP shall be clean, corrosion free and in an operable condition.  GFP is provided to the MCMC for performance of the contract items. The MCMC, a s the custodian, is 
responsible for cleanliness and maintenance to prolong the service life of the equipment. Broken or otherwise unusable equipment shall be repaired or replaced.  
 1.J.2 Office Furniture and Equipment  for Government provided facilities .  Office furniture and 
equipment, to include desks, chairs, filing cabinets, office equipment, telephones, and other related items, shall be furnished by the Government.  Office automated data processing equipment shall be provided by the Government.  The  Government provided computers operate 
on the Marine Corps Enterprise Network (MCEN).  All GFP is for conducting official Government business only.  MCMC provided computers may be utili zed for private MCMC 
business such as a personnel system or benefits system.  MCMC provided computers shall not be allowed to utilize the MCEN, nor should government data be stored on MCMC provided computers.  
 1.J.3  Maintenance Equipment  
 
1.J.3.a  The Government shall provide an initial outfitting of equipment and special tools for the maintenance sites, MCPP -PHIL, other ashore locations, and for the shipboard 
efforts.  Subsequently, the MCMC is responsible for processing requests for equipment and special tools as Contractor Acquired Property IAW H -9.  These items shall be for the 
use of the MCMC.  
1.J.3.b  MCMC shall be responsible for the acquisition of all components of tool chests, sets, kits and other maintenance equipment which are beyond economical repair or lost, per the FAR part 45.  Accountability of such assets shall be maintained through the life cycle of the item.  MCMC shall provide normal maintenance and repair for all GFE listed in Contract  Attachment 6 a with the exception of IT equipment for which other entities 
hold the maintenance responsibility. Supplies and repair parts to perform  this work shall 
be obtained by the MCMC through the DoD supply system per procedures established for maintenance of GFE equipment.  If repairs to equipment are beyond the MCMC's capability, the KO shall be notified; necessary arrangements shall be made to  provide 
required technical or industrial repair services.  If additional tools or equipment are required to conduct maintenance, the COR shall be notified.  MCMC shall be responsible for ensuring all organic equipment requiring calibration is shipped to a  certified 
calibration laboratory in sufficient time to allow the equipment to be recertified and returned to meet production timelines.  Repair parts, subassemblies, assemblies, consumable supplies, and modification kits shall be provided by the Government to support equipment maintenance.  Replenishment actions are the responsibility of the MCMC, using the current APSR.  The Government shall provide automated data processing equipment and programs required to fulfill mandatory management reporting 
M6700425R0007

Page 60 of 425 
 
 and supply maintenance support systems.  Additional requirements or changes desired by 
the MCMC in hardware or software shall be requested in writing to the BICmd Information Systems Management Office (ISMO)/MCSF -BI S-6 Officer.  Required 
petroleum, oil, l ubricants (POL), and fuels shall be provided by the Government. 
 1.J.4  Restrictions.  Government property made available under this contract shall be for use only in the performance of tasks outlined in this SOW, or as otherwise authorized in writing by the Government.  All items purchased and utilized for performance of this contract which are non-consumable shall be added to the appropriate property account.  A letter from the MCMC requesting changes to the organic property accounts must be approved by the Government prior to purchase.  
  
1.K  REFERENCES, PUBLICATIONS, FORMS AND REPORTS  
 1.K.1  References.  The Government shall initially provide all required reference manuals and applicable publications in electronic format to provide for a contract reference library in the designated maintenance facility and aboard each ship.  This librar y shall be furnished to the 
MCMC for their use; all subsequent changes and additions to the library shall be electronically updated by MCMC to maintain currency of publications in accordance with the latest edition of relevant publication regulations (i.e. MCO’s 5215.17D and 5600.31B).  Due to the uniqueness of this program, there are instances where work requirements are not specifically identified by a publication.  Requirements which are not defined by an authorized publication are specifically addressed  in this SOW.  Work requirements not the subject of a publication or outlined in this 
SOW shall be reported to the BICmd KO for resolution.  All recommended changes to existing specifications outlined in this SOW shall be referred to the Government for review.  
  1.K.2  Forms.  All required forms will be provided or approved by the Government with replenishment procedures established on a free issue basis to the MCMC.  New requirements or replenishment of Government forms identified in this SOW (or applicable order s) shall be 
requisitioned through the established supply system by the MCMC. The MCMC shall use electronic forms to the extent they are practical or directed by the Government.  
 
1.K.3  Reports.  MCMC shall develop cyclical reports.  These reports may be Government system generated or the basis of these reports may come from data collected within the Government provided systems.  Further, should the MCMC wish to use reports generat ed from a 
system other than the Government -provided system the data source shall be available to the 
Government as well.  Required reports are listed in the respective section of the SOW and 
identified as CDRLs in Section J of the IDIQ , as specified in the  task order level, which may add 
additional requirements  for the task order . 
 1.K.4  Contract Reference Library.  The MCMC shall make the completed and updated Contract Reference Library available to the Government through a centralized document management system (such as SharePoint or Teams) no later than February 1st each year.  T he naming 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 61 of 425 
 
 convention for electronic files shall align with the designated reference, TAMCN/ID number, 
and/or publication name.   1.K.5 Publication Revisions (to include FSBs, MAMs, SOUMs, etc.).  The MCMC has 30 calendar days, upon any Government notification of publication revision, to assess any revision 
impacts and identify to the Government incremental costs associated with changes to requirements or standards that directly impact the MCPP.  When a publication revision will cause impacts on labor, materials, other direct and/or indirect costs, or require any additional technical direction or contract adjustments by the Government, the MCMC shall provide a detailed impact assessment analysis associated with the publication revision.  Impacts related to a publication revision will be considered and negotiated as an equitable adjustment, when appropriate.   1.L RIGHTS IN DATA  
 1.L.1   The Government asserts ownership of all data associated with the MCPP and this contract.  The MCMC shall provide the Government with access to and the right to use a completed and updated electronic copy of the Contract Documentation and all associ ated 
Technical Files through a centralized document management system (e.g., SharePoint or Teams) on an annual basis, no later than February 1st of each year. All data delivered will remain the property of the Government, with the Government retaining full rights to use, reproduce, modify, and distribute the data as needed.    1.L.2 Any and all financial, administrative, or management information produced in performance of this contract shall be made available (in electronic medium and in the format requested) upon written or verbal request of the Government.  The MCMC shall not own information/data directly associated with the MCPP ; information related to MCPP readiness shall not be distributed or otherwise disclosed without prior Government approval.

SECTION C.2  PROGRAM AND RISK MANAGEMENT  
 
2.A PLANS AND PLANS DATA MANAGEMENT   
 
The Plans Branch is responsible for the development and management of all plans related to the 
Marine Corps Prepositioning Programs. Plans Branch also oversees and manages all level VI data generated by the MCMC ISO the MPF MMC and monitors and conducts quality control of level VI data developed ISO all ashore prepo sites. Plans Branch also plays a major role in the development of guidelines and command policies governing the marking of all prepositioned assets.     Following the completion of the Tailoring effort, led and managed by HQMC DC I&L and approved by DC PP&O, Blount Island Command is responsible for developing Squadron Level Plans for the MPF program based off the newly approved Prepositioning Objective (PO ). These 
squadron level plans should be completed NLT 180 days prior to Production Day (P -Day) of the 
first ship of the MMC.  Unlike the afloat program whose timelines are mainly driven by shipyard 
requirements, ashore sites have greater flexibility as to when assets will be worked and shipped 
to those sites.     There are multiple types of plans generated at the squadron and individual ship levels.  Squadron level plans are developed OOA P -180 and are only developed once following a Tailoring Cycle. 
Squadron level plans are posted onto MCPIC OOA P -150 from the fir st ship’s production of the 
MMC so the MCMC can review them for situational awareness. The current squadron level plans posted onto MCPIC in the MCPIC Prepositioning Planning System (PPS) application are as follows:  
 
• Squadron Overview  
• Squadron Plan – Provides a rollup of the planned qty for each TAMCN  
• Ship Spread Plan – Found in the MCPIC Prepositioning Planning System (PPS) 
application. Provides a listing of how the squadron planned qty is distributed to the individual ships   
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 82 of 425 
 
 • Major Subordinate Element (MSE) Plan – Provides a listing of how the squadron planned 
qty is distributed across the MSEs.  
• MSE Plan By Ship – Provides a listing of the qty for a particular TAMCN that is 
assigned to each of the MSEs by ship  
• Ship by MSE – Same as above only the listing is broke out by ship for each of the MSEs  
• Capability Set (CAPSET) Letter – The CAPSET Letter is a listing of capabilities and their contents which are intended to be packaged, packed and marked in a specified manner and special stowed aboard the MPS  
• Armory Weapons Squadron Plan – Listing of weapons or optics quantities traditionally stored in the armory aboard the vessels and their associated weapons system. This listing is broken out by ship. 
• CMS Container Spread -   Listing of CCI equipment quantities traditionally stored in a 
CMS designated container or in the armory with their associated end item. This listing is broken out by ship. 
• Sustainment Blocks – Listing of items and their quantities divided by their individual blocks by ship. Examples would be the Petroleum, Oils and Lubricants (POL) Block, Battery Block and Class IV Fortification Block. These blocks are packaged and packed as  
a single capability, marked appropriately and stored aboard the specified ships within the plan.  
• Class I Rations Block – While Class I Rations are considered part of sustainment, they have their own plan due to the format of this block.  
• Container Estimate Worksheet – This worksheet is developed during the tailoring 
process, refined during the development of Squadron Level Plans and adjusted, when 
necessary, throughout the MMC. This worksheet serves as a tool to track, manage and 
monitor t he number of containers required to pack specified capabilities. This worksheet 
is a quick ready reference that alerts planners of when a ship may be over its TEU capacity or when there may be available TEU slots.  Individual detailed level ship plans are developed at P -150, staffed, reviewed and provided to the MCMC NLT P -90.  
• Load Type Plan – Provides a listing of planned load methods for all TAMCN/EC items 
being loaded onto a specified ship.  Examples are Armory, Square Load, Load onto, Put 
Into, Mobile Loaded, Container, and Hitched.  This plan also has links to Equipment Lif e 
Cycle Management.   
• Association Plan -  Provides detailed load and configuration requirements for each 
TAMCN/EC loaded onto a specified ship.  
• Logistical Set Report – Provides a detailed list of planned Children items stowed away from Parent item.  
• Naval Support Element (NSE) Container Plan -   Provides planned container quantities 
and packed equipment sets per container.  
• NSE Weapons Armory Plan – Provides detailed listing of planned weapons and serial numbers for NSE.  
• NSE Watercraft Workbook -   Provides detailed listing of planned watercraft.  
• Navy Battery Block – Provides listing of planned battery block for Navy equipment. 
• Naval Construction Element (NCE) OPP Block – Detailed listing of all equipment and supplies planned in support of Navy Offload Preparation Party operations.   
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 83 of 425 
 
 • NCE Container Plan - Provides planned container quantities and packed equipment sets 
per container. 
• CESE Workbook – A detailed workbook, similar to the Association Plan, that is provided by the Navy for both NSE and NCE rolling stock and provides mobile load plans, serial number and cab configurations and any special stow requirements. 
• CMS Container Plan – Provides detailed list of all COMSEC material that will be stowed 
aboard ship in a single consolidated container or armory. 
• Armory Plan – Provides a detailed listing of USMC ground weapons to be stowed within 
a specific ship’s armory.  
• E00712BA_E00712BD Plan – Provides detailed installation requirements for the different versions of the MCTAGS on MTVR and LVSR platforms.  
  The MCMC has a major role in the Command's ability to achieve mission success in plans and data accuracy. The MCMC is responsible for ensuring approved Squadron and Ship level plans are adhered to in order to properly configure and stow the stated quantity of equipment and supplies aboard specified vessels. Second only to the equipment readiness, is the responsibility of the MCMC to provide data in a complete, accurate and timely manner.    The MCMC shall utilize government provided information management systems, data bases, and software systems to review, download, manage, store, and modify Maritime Prepositioning Ships planning related data as applicable. The existing system, Marine Corps Platform Integration Center (MCPIC) IPACK, will be manually populated by the MCMC as detailed in Command Bulletin (CmdBul) 3162 Data Standards.     MCMC shall conduct pRFID PACMAN runs at a minimum of twice per week to ensure current locations of equipment and containers are reflected in MCPIC iTRACK.    
2.A.1 Plans Management  
 2.A.1.a The MCMC shall adhere to the government provided Container Estimate Worksheet when loading/packing containerized equipment and materials into twenty-foot and twenty-foot equivalent unit (TEU) ISO containers.  The government provided Container Estimate Worksheet is a squadron level plan and is posted to MCPIC NLT 120 calendar days prior to the first ship of the MMC. The MCMC shall notify the Government Plans Branch of any deviations from the Government Container Worksheet.    2.A.1.b The MCMC shall adhere to the government provided Squadron and Detailed Ship Level Plans IAW MMC Execution Milestones.  Changes to the Squadron or Detailed Ship Level Plans are executed and posted within MCPIC once approved by the Government staff.  Email notifications are generated out of MCPIC and sent to subscribers of the MCMC.

2.B  MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT

 This Section sets forth the requirements and responsibilities of the MCMC for Maintenance 
Management.  Maintenance Management is the set of procedures aimed at efficiently and effectively using various resources in the preservation and maintenance of equipment.  Maintenance management integrates policies, programs and manual/automated procedures that support the synchronization and oversight of maintenance production and engineering across all commodities.  It coordinates the integration of supporting logis tics 
functions and operations to provide flexibility and responsiveness in support of established war fighting functions.  Maintenance management efforts impact quality maintenance engineering through training, data collection, reporting, analysis, and eva luation via Automated Information 
Systems (AIS), publication management and modification/configuration control. Maintenance management includes but is not limited to; continuous planning, coordination, analysis, evaluations, and improvement actions focused on maintenance administration, resource management, maintenance operations, supply support, equipment information management, quality control, and equipment operational reporting requirements.   Maintenance Management is supported by AIS.  Together, they provide management visibility while simultaneously collating maintenance analysis information for item management.  Understanding and executing Marine Corps policies is essential for effective maintenance management required to ensure mission accomplishmen t and auditability.  
  The MCMC shall execute the Marine Corps ground equipment maintenance management program to support the organization, management, and integration of ground equipment maintenance capabilities in support of the Operating Forces. The MCMC Maintenance Managemen t Office Manager shall be responsible for all “Maintenance Management Officer” 
responsibilities IAW MCO 4790.2 and CmdO 4790.1A for both ashore and afloat programs unless otherwise specifically stated as a Government responsibility.  The MCMC shall manage 
the eight primary maintenance management functional areas and the Using Unit Account Manager program.  The MCMC shall use the guidance contained within this Section in conjunction with applicable Marine Corps Orders, directives, and manuals to manage all maintenance efforts required under this Contract.  The maintenance management responsibilities 
of the MCMC include:  Maintenance Administration, Personnel and Training, Records and Reports, Publications Control, Equipment Availability, PMCS and CM, Supply S upport, and 
Maintenance Related Programs.  Detailed descriptions and requirements for each of the eight functional areas are addressed in MCO 4790.2 and its references.  Marine Corps technical directives provide policy, procedures, and routine guidance, ex cept where modified by this SOW 
to meet unique program requirements.  
 The Maintenance Management requirements herein shall be performed in accordance with Government approved production and delivery schedules at all locations governed by this contract.  Products shall conform to the specifications described within this secti on.  Quality 
assurance measurements shall be governed by the performance requirements and standards associated with this section and specifications described within Section 2.D.   2.B.1 Maintenance Administration

days after contract award; thereafter, the appointment letter shall be submitted NLT 5 business days after a change in personnel.

the Government NLT 10 business days after contract award; thereafter, the Delegation of Authority Letter shall be submitted to the Government NLT 10 business days after the arrival of a new hire requiring authorization.  The MCMC shall submit an updated letter 
to the Gove rnment annually NLT 10 business days after January 1st and July 1st of each 
calendar year.  
 2.B.1.d  Since the Government and the MCMC are required to be jointly certified by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), the MCMC shall create and maintain ISO Work Instructions, Management Procedures, and forms in lieu of Desktop Procedures and  Turnover Folders as described in MCO -4790.2 (Chapter 1, 3.a).

after contract award; thereafter the MCMC shall submit to the Government any periodic changes to the Internal Inspection Schedule prior to implementation and NLT 5 business days after July 1st of e ach year.

4790.1B. Internal Inspection Results shall be submitted to the Government NLT 10 business days after completion of inspection as identified in the internal inspection schedule.

1007) for discrepancies identified during Internal Inspections IAW MCO 4790.2 (Chapter 
1 C. (1).e) and CmdO 4790.1B.  The MCMC shall submit the POCA to the Government NLT 21 business days after the conclusion of the inspection. Timelines established in the  
POCA will be adhered to and discrepancies corrected on or before the established due date.

2.B.2 Supply Support 
 
2.B.2.a  The MCMC shall place newly fielded equipment into service IAW Fielding Plans and other equipment-specific publications (TMs, SIs, etc.), MCO 4790.2 (Chapter 1,4.c) and CmdO 4790.1B.  2.B.2.b  The MCMC shall establish warranty coordination procedures that identify ME under warranty IAW MCO 4790.2 (Chapter 1.4.c, Chapter 3.6.b.2.e, Chapter 4.10), MCO 4855.10C (4.b.1.g page 2), CmdO 4790.1B, MCO 4105.2A (all), and NAVMC 4855.1 (All).  
    2.B.2.c  The MCMC shall adhere to procedures for Contracted Logistics Support (CLS) items requiring service IAW MCO 4105.2A (All), MCO 4790.2 (Chapter 3 1.b.1; 8.a.(3); 8.b.(1).(d)),MCO 4200.33 (All), MCO 4790.25 (4.(b.)(6).(d).3), and CmdO4790.1B.  2.B.2.d  The MCMC shall establish and manage the receipt, transfer, storage, organization, and validation of repair parts within a maintenance layette, also known as parts bin, ensuring efficient and accurate operations in alignment with Marine Corps maintenance procedures outlined in CmdO4790.1B and UM 4000-125 Volume 3. The MCMC shall implement processes to streamline the flow of parts from receipt through storage in layettes, ensuring parts are readily available for application to associated military equ ipment. The MCMC shall maintain a high level of organization and 
accountability to prevent errors in parts tracking and ensure the timely and accurate parts application and debrief of parts to maintenance tasks. Additionally, the MCMC shall conduct bi- week ly validations of all parts, ensuring compliance with inventory standards 
and promptly addressing and correcting discrepancies.  
(i)  The MCMC shall establish and organize the necessary stage sub -inventories to 
support the receipt and transfer of repair parts to the designated layette sub-inventories. The stage sub-inventory will serve as an initial holding area for parts before they are assigned to the corresponding layette for specific Service Requests (SRs). Parts in stage sub-inventories shall be properly organized, tracked, and moved to the appropriate layette sub-inventory without delay.   (ii) The MCMC shall establish and organize layette sub-inventories to ensure proper storage and management of repair parts. Each layette locator will be designated for specific Service Requests (SR) and will be clearly labeled and organized.   (iii) Upon receipt of requisitioned repair parts, the MCMC shall be responsible for transferring parts from the stage sub-inventory to the corresponding layette sub-

 inventory. This process shall include ensuring that all parts are properly tagged 
with the corresponding SR number, and that the correct locator number is assigned to each item. The MCMC shall ensure that this transfer is done efficiently, without delay, a nd with strict accountability for all parts received.  
 (vi) The MCMC shall maintain the organization of all parts within the layette sub -
inventory. Small parts will be stored together in clearly marked bins (locators), while larger parts will be stored in designated storage areas within the layette. Each part will be tagged with its corresponding SR number. The MCMC shall ensure that all parts related to a specific SR are stored together in the appropriate and dedicated bin (locator), minimizing the risk of misplacement or misuse. Parts that have been removed f rom equipment for maintenance shall also be stored in 
the SR -specific parts bin, ensuring that no parts are used for any task other than 
the one for which they were requisitioned.  (v)  The MCMC shall establish a process for returning parts to the layette sub-inventories if they are not applied (debriefed) within the same business day. Any parts removed from inventory that remain unused by the end of the business day shall be promptl y returned to the appropriate layette, ensuring accurate tracking 
and maintaining inventory integrity.  (vi) The MCMC shall be responsible for conducting bi -weekly validations of all 
parts stored in the layette sub- inventories. This process involves comparing 
physical inventory counts against the quantities recorded in the GCSS -MC system 
to ensure all parts are accounted for and properly applied (debriefed) to the 
corresponding SRs. Any discrepancies identified during this validation process must be swiftly corrected. The MCMC shall maintain detailed records of all validation activities, including the results  of each inventory check and any 
adjustments made.  
 (vii) The MCMC shall ensure that all activities related to the receipt, transfer, storage, and validation of parts are properly documented. The MCMC shall establish a process for reporting discrepancies, damaged items, or parts that are no longer required for SRs. This process shall include the timely identification, documentation, and notification of such issues for appropriate action. The MCMC shall promptly correct any discrepancies and report the receipt of damaged parts using a Supply Discrepancy Repor t (SDR) process.
