 4.C.9.a  The MCMC shall submit requests (voucher requests) for property record 
adjustments IAW MCO 4400.201 and MCO P4400.151 ( CDRL 5035) .  All property 
record adjustment requests, regardless of dollar value and/or type of gain or loss, shall be routed to the Government (Attention:  “Program” OIC) via voucher request form for approval prior to transacting in the APSR utilizing the Government pr ovided format for 
the voucher request ( SOW Attachment C.4 -5).  In cases of physical loss adjustments 
(DIC D9_) where Marine Corps property was lost, damaged, or destroyed, a DD Form 200 shall accompany the voucher request that is routed to the Government for approval (reference 4.C.8.a) IAW MCO 4400.201 (Vol 17, Chapt ers 2 and 7) or upon the 
discretion of the Government that a DD 200 is required. All voucher requests forwarded to the Government shall be endorsed by the MCMC's Supply Manager and shall include results of causative research, identification of root cause f or accountability imbalance, 
justification for recommended property record adjustment transactions, and the following fields:  Applicable AAC, recommended Document Identifier Code (DIC) to make inventory adjustments, TACMN (where applicable), NIIN, Nomencl ature, Serial Number 
(where applicable), SAC, CIC, Quantity, Unit Price, Extended Price (of recommended APSR adjustment).  Voucher requests shall be submitted within five business days of identification of inventory discrepancy, and the voucher request inf ormation shall be 
accurate IAW physical OH inventory, APSR property records, APSR historical transaction information (related to causative research).  
 4.C.9.b The MCMC shall route all APSR generated Money Value Gain Loss (MVGL) notices to the Government for endorsement IAW MCO 4400.201 and MCO P4400.151.  
The MCMC shall ensure routed MVGLs include a copy of the corresponding voucher requests (and DD Form 200 where applicable) that were previously approved and 
endorsed by the Government, serving as the authorization to transact the property record adjustment  in the APSR.  MVGLs shall be routed to the Government within 5 business 
days of processing the adjustment transaction within GCSS -MC. 
 4.C.9.c The MCMC shall complete required supply transactions associated with the Monthly Field Change (MFC) IAW UM4000- 125 and monthly GCSS -MC Operations 
Center (GOC) MFC processing guidance.  MFCs result from updates to core GCSS -MC 
data, usually associated with IMO attributes such as the serial control flag, IB tracking flag, and/or unit of issue.  Government will provide detailed guidance for each MFC based on the pre -MFC notifications receiv ed.  When this occurs, the MCMC shall 
process appropriate APSR transactions required to ensure the property is accurately loaded back to property records under new attribute values.  Voucher requests associated with MFCs shall be routed to the Government to document the requested property record adjustments, and Gover nment approval of the voucher requests shall be obtained prior to 
transacting property record adjustments.  In the case of MFC, there will not be a need for an accompanying DD Form 200, unless Marine Corps property is physically lost, damaged, or destroyed.  Voucher requests associated with MFC shall be submitted by the MCMC to the Government within five business days of completion of MFC cycle.  Subsequently, GCSS -MC inventory adjustment transactions shall be completed by the 
MCMC within three business days of Government (voucher) approval.  The MCMC shall

 ensure the GCSS -MC transactions are accurate (DIC, quantity, NSN) IAW the 
Government approved voucher request. 
 
4.C.10 The MCMC shall retain accurate and complete supply documentation, records, and files IAW MCO 4400.201, MCO P4400.151 and UM 4000-125.  4.C.11  The MCMC shall maintain the BICmd NTV Program IAW MCO P11240.106D, NAVFAC P -307, CmdO 11240.1, and GSA Vehicle Lease Program (VLP) MOU.  A 
Government representative will formally serve as Fleet Manager, and MCMC shall maintain and execute the operations of the program with Fleet Manager oversight.  Program shall include NTV, dispatching of assets, accountability, maintenance, accident prevention, licensing, utilization tracking, and accident reporting.  The MCMC shall utilize DPAS for asset accountability and utilize the Marine Corps -approved Federal Fleet Management Information 
System (FMIS), “Fleet Focus”, for life cycle management of GME fleet assets.  The MCMC shall perform, or obtain from a third-party, emergent equipment recovery for Government Furnished Equipment and/or Military Equipment traveling to or from inspection and repair facilities outside the confines of Marine Corps Support Facility -Blount Island.  
 4.C.11.a The MCMC shall operate and maintain Government-furnished Non- Tactical 
Vehicles (NTV) IAW MCO P11240.106D, NAVFAC P-307, CmdO 11240.1, and GSA Vehicle Lease Program (VLP) MOU . 
 4.C.11.b The MCMC shall implement and manage a Non- Tactical Vehicle Licensing 
Program IAW CmdO 11240.1, MCO P11240.106D, MCO 11240.66, TM 11240-15/3, and TM 11275-15/4.   
 4.C.12
 MCPP -N Specific:  Monthly, the MCMC shall reconcile MMV420 GCSS -MC all classes 
of supply data against NEIMS UICs, conduct causative research (if necessary) contact NDLO/MEB counterparts, and update the GCSS -MC to reflect.  The MCMC shall submit a 
detailed report to the government electronically ( CDRL 5036) .  The MCMC shall utilize GCSS -
MC and NEIMS AIS including reports produced by NDLO/MEB.  
4.D MMC SUPPLY PRODUCTION OPERATIONS  
 4.D.1 The MCMC shall manage intermediate level supply accounts to support BICmd consumer level accounts and the MMC with consumables and secondary reparables IAW MCO 4400.201, MCO P4400.151, MCO P4450.7, MCO 4790.2, and UM 4000-125.  Intermediate level supply accounts include a General Account (AAC MMV200) and a Reparable Issue Point (AAC MMFAU1), both located on MCSF- BI. 
 4.D.2  The MCMC shall operate the MCSF-BI Defense Fuel Support Point (DFSP) IAW DOD 4140.25- M, DLA-Energy P1, DLA-Energy P2, DLA-Energy P7, and other established DLA-
Energy procedures for capitalized fuel storage.  The MCSF -BI DFSP is Government-
owned/Contractor- operated (GOCO).  MCMC operations shall include the ordering of fuel, 
receiving of fuel, monitoring of fuel levels, recording and reporting of fuel consumption, 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 145 of 425 
 
 inventory management, recycling, safeguarding, and distribution of fuel.  The MCMC shall 
ensure adequate fuel supplies are on hand at all times to support operations with levels maintained IAW DLA -E procedures.  Accurate and complete DD Form 2921 ( CDRL 5037)  shall 
be submitted daily (for previous day's consumption/inventory levels).  Accurate and complete DD Form 1348- 8 shall be submitted within two business days after the end of month.  MCPP -
PHIL is exempt from this requirement.  
 4.D.3  The MCMC shall submit an Attainment Deficiency Report (CDRL 5038 ) identifying ME 
deficiencies for the upcoming ship or ashore program in the MPF Maintenance Cycle (MMC) IAW the Detailed Ship Plans, O/H inventory resident in APSR, and physical O/H inventory.  Government will provide requirements document (Detailed Ship Plans), which identifies the load out/spread for each respective ship, to the MCMC NLT 90 calendar days prior to start of ships' production periods.  Subsequently, the MCMC shall submit  the Attainment Deficiency Report to 
the Government (Attn: Supply Branch) NLT 75 calendar days prior to the commencement of the respective ships' production period (P -75), or 15 calendar days after receiving the Detailed Ship 
Plans from the Government, whi chever is later.  The ME Attainment Deficiency Report shall 
highlight equipment shortfalls that exist when comparing available O/H assets to the load out “spread” quantities in the Detailed Ship Plans.  Report shall include data elements for:  TAMCN, NIIN,  nomenclature, Stores Account Code (SAC), quantity short/required to attain, unit price, 
and extended price (of deficient quantity).  When determining ME attainment deficiencies for the ship or ashore program  in MMC production, consideration shall be given to available program -
wide excesses and/or on hand assets filling lower priority requirements that can be used to fill immediate MMC/ship PO requirements.    4.D.4  (RESERVED)  
 4.D.5  The MCMC shall prepare the shipboard sustainment blocks and containerized/mobile loaded assets during MMC production, preparing materiel for MPF backload at the conclusion of the ships' production cycles IAW Detailed Ship Plans.  
4.D.5.a The MCMC shall download and unpack all containers and mobile loads IAW TM 4790- 14/2, Government -approved Production Schedules, and Government -provided 
Ship/Production Planning Considerations.  4.D.5.b The MCMC shall inventory downloaded sustainment blocks and containerized assets IAW APSR Property Records, Government -approved Production Schedules, 
MCPIC embarkation records, TM 4790- 14/2, MCO 4400.201, MCO P4400.151, and 
Detailed Ship Plans.   
 4.D.5.c The MCMC shall adjust APSR property records to reflect accurate OH inventory of downloaded sustainment blocks IAW Section 4.C. “Inventory Management”.  4.D.5.d The MCMC shall manage shelf life of sustainment block assets and materiel IAW Section 4.C. “Inventory Management” during MMC production, to include screening for shelf life expiration, obtaining extensions where possible, and determining 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 146 of 425 
 
 materiel that is eligible for another afloat phase versus materiel that shall be replaced 
IAW Detailed Ship Plans.   4.D.5.e  The MCMC shall conduct serviceability inspections of sustainment block contents and containerized assets/materiel (to include CBRN equipment), IAW Section 4.C. “Inventory Management”.  Specific to all inspections of CBRN equipment, the MCMC shall record the inspection results using the Government -provided CBRN 
Inspection Log ( SOW Attachment C.4 -6), which shall be retained on file for a minimum 
of six years and available for Government review.  
 4.D.5.f  The MCMC shall conduct stock rotation of sustainment block contents during MMC production IAW Section 4.C “Inventory Management” and Detailed Ship Plans.  
 
(i) The MCMC shall conduct MRE stock rotation with DLA Troop Support (DLA -TS) IAW Detailed Ship Plans and the Operational Rations PBA Annex 
between DLA and HQMC (LF).  MREs contained in the CL I prepositioned blocks embarked aboard MPF shall be downloaded during MMC production in order to rotate the stocks with DLA -TS and acquire replacement stocks that have 
adequate shelf life to last through the scheduled afloat phase.  Rations/MREs shall possess 33 months or more until end of service life at the time of MMC embarkation aboard MPF IAW MCO 4000.58.  The quantity of MREs obtained from DLA and back loaded aboard respective ships during MMC production phase shall be IAW Detailed Ship Plans.  
 
4.D.5.g  The MCMC shall track and report prepositioned sustainment block excesses and 
deficiencies IAW Detailed Ship Plans, TM 4790- 14/2, as well as Official Government 
Guidance provided via Ship/Production Planning Considerations.  The MCMC shall provide the r eport NLT 75 calendar days prior to commencement of ship production, or 
15 calendar days after receiving the Detailed Ship Plans from the Government, whichever is later, concurrent with the ME Attainment Deficiency Report  (CDRL 
5040) .    Sustainment b locks include Class I Rations, Class III Packaged (P), Class IV 
Engineering/Construction materiel and lumber, and Class IX (consumable repair parts, batteries, and Secondary Reparables); Preservation, Packaging, Packing and Marking (P3&M) block; and Offloa d Preparation Party (OPP) block IAW respective ships’ 
Detailed Ship Plans.  Sustainment Block Status Report, which shall be part of the ME Attainment Deficiency Report submitted at P -75, shall identify excesses and deficiencies 
relative to Ship Spread cont ained in the Detailed Ship Plans found in the MCPIC 
Prepositioning Planning System (PPS) application, with amplifying guidance in the Ship Planning Considerations, and it will include identification of which sustainment block the excess/deficiency applies to, NIIN, Nomenclature, U/I, quantity O/H, quantity excess or 
deficient, U/P, extended price of excess or deficient quantity, SAC, CEC, end item application where appropriate, and remarks that include requested disposition action (MRP, DLA -DS / DRMO, WIR) for excesses or sourcing plan for deficiencies. 
 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP

 (i) The MCMC shall requisition/source approved material for downloaded 
sustainment blocks IAW NAVMC 2907 (PO), Detailed Ship Plans, MCO 
4400.201, MCO 4000.58, MCO P4400.151, TM 4790- 14/2, MCO 4400.16, MCO 
3000.17, and UM 4000- 125.  Ship allowance quantities (quantity to be attained 
and backloaded during the MMC) are provided in the respective Government -
provided Detailed Ship Plans, provided to the MCMC 90 calendar days prior to the start of ships' production period.  For those susta inment block deficiencie s 
without a GA O/H quantity, requisitions shall be submitted for the upcoming ship in the MMC NLT 60 calendar days prior to the start of the ship's production cycle (after receiving Government approval), or 10 calendar days after receiving Government approval of Attainment Deficiency Report, whichever is later.  Any sustainment block materiel that has constraints associated with early attainment negatively affecting the shelf life during the afloat phase (i.e. CL I MREs, CL III POL, CL IX batteries) shall b e an exception to the aforementioned P -60 
requisition timeline.  In these cases, materiel shall be attained at the latest possible date that still allows for MCMC receipt, inventory, P3&M, containerize, mark, etc. during the MMC in order to meet the ship backload timeline, thereby maximizing remaining shelf life for the afloat phase.  Materiel shall be sourced IAW Section 4.B.7.  
 (ii) The MCMC shall conduct rollback or disposition of excess/obsolete inventory from downloaded consumable sustainment blocks IAW Section 4.C. “Inventory Management” NLT the end of the respective ship’s production cycle.  
 
4.D.5.h  The MCMC shall load and secure MMC -completed mobile loads onto/into 
designated Principal End Items IAW Government -provided Mobile Load Association 
Plans, which are part of the Detailed Plans in MCPIC.  Mobile loads shall be secured IAW Section C.9  Port Operations.  Mobile loads shall be secured as to allow Lift -
On/Lift- Off (LO/LO), Roll -On/Roll -Off (RO/RO), and not hinder the proper operations 
of the Principle End Item. 
 4.D.6 The MCMC shall conduct supply operations associated with Sets, Kits, Outfits, and Tools (SKOT)

4.D.5.h  The MCMC shall load and secure MMC -completed mobile loads onto/into 
designated Principal End Items IAW Government -provided Mobile Load Association 
Plans, which are part of the Detailed Plans in MCPIC.  Mobile loads shall be secured IAW Section C.9  Port Operations.  Mobile loads shall be secured as to allow Lift -
On/Lift- Off (LO/LO), Roll -On/Roll -Off (RO/RO), and not hinder the proper operations 
of the Principle End Item. 
 4.D.6 The MCMC shall conduct supply operations associated with Sets, Kits, Outfits, and Tools (SKOT) and SL -3 requirements during MMC/ashore production IAW the Government approved 
Production Schedule, equipment TMs, and SL -3 Component Stock Lists.  
 4.D.6.a  The MCMC shall conduct SL -3 inventories of SKOTs that are needed in support 
of the MMC production period, as identified in the Detailed Plans “spread” within MCPIC.  Inventories shall be conducted IAW MCO 4400.201, UM 4000- 125, CmdO 
4790.1, and respective equipment TMs/SL -3 Component Stock Lists .  The MCMC shall 
place a copy of completed SL -3 inventory list in each SKOT upon completion of the 
physical inventory during production.  Inventory lists shall be accurate IAW physical O/H inventory.  In addition, the MCMC shall upload inventory lists into GCSS -MC for 
auditability and accountability. Ashore program SKOTS inventories shall be included in the respective annual/quarterly production plans specific to the programs.  
 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 148 of 425 
 
 4.D.6.b The MCMC shall requisition SL -3 deficiencies of ME and SKOTs to meet MMC 
production and backload timelines IAW MCO 4400.201, CmdO 4790.1, UM 4000- 125, 
Detailed Ship Plans, SL -3 Component Stock Lists, equipment TMs, and completed SL -3 
inventories.  To increase the likelihood of producing SL -3 complete assets during 
production, the MCMC shall review the “SL -3 Deficiency Report” from the ship’s 
previous MMC, and if the same ME or SKOTs are being planned for the current MMC 
production period, the MCMC  shall ensure the SL -3 are ordered within the first 45 
calendar days of the production period.  Likewise, the MCMC shall requisition any SL -3 
deficiencies discovered during the conduct of LTIs and completion of SL -3 inventories as 
part of MMC production.  The MCMC shall ensure all prepositioned Military Equipment (ME) and Sets.  Kits, Outfits and Tools (SKOT) meet the following stock criteria for components:  
 
(i)  Contain all components identified in the applicable data as CEC/CRIT 3, 4, 5 and 6 components, unless otherwise authorized by the government.  
(ii)  Meet a minimum SL -3 Component Stock Level (CSL) of 80%.  
 
MCPP -N and MCPP -PHIL Specific:  All MCPP -N and MCPP -PHIL SKOTs 
shall be 100% validated prior to shipment to Norway or the Philippines.  The term validated is defined as identifying shortages, placing deficiencies on order, receiving deficiencies, and placi ng in SKOT prior to shipment to Norway or the 
Philippines.  Government authorization is required for shipments not SL -3 
complete by appropriate MCO or applicable TM.  
 
4.D.6.c The MCMC shall provide an SL -3 Deficiency Report ( CDRL 50 41) for the 
ship/ashore site in production NLT 14 calendar  days prior to the end of the production 
cycle (Px -14).  At a minimum, this report shall contain the following data elements for 
SL-3 shortfalls:  TAMCN (if applicable), Nomenclature, and NIIN of Parent ME that has 
the SL -3 shortage;  NIINs, Nomenclatures, Quantities of SL -3 Allowances, Quantities of 
SL-3 OH, Deficiencies, U/ P, T/P, CEC/CRIT, SAC, and Document Numbers of 
Requisitions  f or SL -3 Deficiencies.  Provision of this report from the MCMC to the 
Government will allow for Government review and feedback on whether to load the SL -3 
deficient end item, keep outstanding requisitions for SL -3 open or cancel them, and what 
to do with the SL -3 upon receipt (hold O/H in the GA or forward to the ship).   
 
4.D.7  The MCMC shall conduct set assembly marking for component parts of disassembled items IAW CmdO 4035.1.  Marking shall be completed prior to MCMC declaration of respective sets as production complete and ready for Government inspection IAW Government -approved 
Production Schedule.  Set marking is used to easily identify the association between separate components and/or containers of sets.  Assets such as the TAFDS, AAFS, HERS, EFK, etc., which consist of multiple disparate containers, require set markings.  
   Marking structure shall consist of “BIC”, followed by TAMCN of parent end item (B0685), followed by locally assigned four -digit set number that is unique to that particular set/parent end 
item amongst total sets within the program (i.e. 0003), and finally followed by the assigned 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 149 of 425 
 
 number for that particular piece of separate component or container relative to total number of 
separate pieces for the parent end item (i.e. 7 of 30).  Example: "BICB06850003 / 7 of 30" (container 7 of 30 total containers for set #3 of the B0685)  
 4.D.8  The MCMC shall conduct container packing operations IAW  MCO 4450.14, MCO 
4690.1, MCO P4030.31, MCO P4030.21, MCO P4030.33, MCO 4400.201 (Vol 14), TM 4790-14/2, MIL -STD -129, IMDG Code, 49 CFR Part 178, UN Performance Orientation Packaging 
(POP) standards, CmdO 4035.1, and CmdBul 3162.  "Container packing operations" shall include P3&M actions; loading of material into cabinets, bins, or other smaller containers; generation of locator files and packing lists; container marking and labeling relative to container contents; and associated data entry for warehousing/embarkation.  The MCMC shall conduct container packing IAW the Government -provided Container Worksheet and related deviation 
reporting requirements outlined in Section C.9 Port Operations.  Cont ainer contents shall be 
accurate IAW the Detailed Ship Plans for each respective ship.  
 
4.D.8.a The MCMC shall generate and affix container packing lists identifying container contents.  Container packing lists shall be accurate with container contents and MCPIC data.  Packing lists shall be placed on inside and outside of one door for each c ontainer, 
with outside list protected from natural elements.   The MCMC shall complete the container packing list prior to placing the security seal on the container.  The MCMC shall maintain electronic container packing lists so that the Government can ac cess, view, 
sort, and share the data with applicable stakeholders.   4.D.8.b The MCMC shall secure and seal the doors of completed containers with serialized High Security Seal IAW ISO 17712:2013.  The seal shall be affixed 
immediately upon Government approval to close/seal container.  Subsequently, the MCMC shall enter accurate seal data into MCPIC for all container seals.   
 
4.D.9  The MCMC shall conduct P3&M operations in support of MMC production IAW DoD 4140.65- M, MCO P4030.31, MCO 4030.40, MCO 4030.33, MCO P4030.21, MCO 4400.201 
(Vol 14), applicable equipment MIL -SPEC and TMs, MIL -STD -2073- 1, NAVSUP P700- CNP, 
UN POP, and TM 4790- 14/2.  P3&M operations shall be conducted IAW Government -approved 
Production Schedules.  Requirements for P3&M include ME/PEIs, SKOTs, containerized assets, mobile loads, and consumable sustainment blocks.  Embarked rolling stock/vehicles shall be preserved in a modified Level B drive -away state, unique to the requirements of respective 
commodities for which the deprocessing demands of Level A preservation would bear significantly on the operational readiness and ability to rapidly offload/deploy the  assets.  
Weapons, electrical parts and components, communication assets, and optics equipment shall be Level A packed.  Mobile loads shall be protected from the elements.  Stenciled weight markings on containers shall be accurate IAW scale house records and actual physical weight of loaded containers.  Packing material shall be marked IAW MIL -STD 129, MIL -STD 130, 49 CFR, 
IMDG Code, and the UN POP standards.  4.D.10 The MCMC shall conduct logistics support operations for downloaded weapons during MMC production, maintaining accountability throughout the process.  
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP

4.D.10.a The MCMC shall conduct joint inventories of all weapons downloaded from 
MPF during the MMC prior to shipment to LOGCOM for inspection and repair, as well as upon weapons returning from LOGCOM for MPF backload, ensuring accurate accountability for all weapons is maintained.  Weapons downloaded from MPF during 
MMC and weapons returned from LOGCOM in support of MMC backload shall be stored in the BICmd Temporary Weapons Facility when direct shipment between BICmd (after ship download and prior to ship backload) and LOGCOM, and vice versa, is not feasible.  Joint inventories shall be completed upon download from MPF prior to storing in the MCSF -BI Temporary Armory Facility or prior to shipping to LOGCOM for PMCS 
(in cases of direct shipment).  Joint inventories shall be completed within 24 hours of receiving PMCS -completed weapons back from LOGCOM.  
 
(i) The MCMC shall nominate, in writing to the Government, primary and alternate Armory Custodians for the MCSF -BI Temporary Armory Facility.  
Custodians shall be selected so that at least one custodian is available at all times during working hours.  Armory Custodian nomination letters ( CDRL 5042)  shall 
be provided prior to contract execution, and within 24 hours of personnel changes.  The MCMC shall adhere to MCO standards with respect to AA&E access and handling when nominating custodians.  (ii) The MCMC shall nominate, in writing to the Government, designated personnel for access to the BICmd Temporary Weapons Facility.  Neither the Government nor MCMC representatives shall access the facility without the presence of the others' designated representatives.  Armory Access nomination 
letters ( CDRL 5043)  provided prior to contract execution and within 24 hours of 
personnel changes.  
 
4.D.10.b The MCMC shall inventory, replace, and replenish weapons SL -3 as required 
IAW Section 4.A. and 4.B.  Requisitions for weapons SL -3 deficiencies shall be 
submitted NLT 15 calendar days after the start of production.  4.D.10.c The MCMC shall complete appropriate APSR transactions of weapons shipped to/from LOGCOM or other repair facility IAW UM 4000- 125, MCO 4400.201, and 
Detailed Ship Plans .  LOGCOM MDMC conducts Field Level Maintenance PMCS 
services on BICmd weapons.  As such, upon ship downloads, the MCMC shall transfer the weapons to MDMC.  Depending upon the “spread” requirement for the respective production period, the MCMC shall submit  requisitions for replacement weapons, using 
an RDD that is within 2 -3 weeks of Px -day for the given production period.  APSR 
transfer transactions shall be completed the same calendar day as weapon outbound shipments or completing physical inventory of received (inbound/arriving) shipments.  All weapons sent to LOGCOM for maintenance shall be transferred from property records, regardless if required to be returned for backload or not within the next few months.  Lastly, additional weapons, not already on BICmd property records may be 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP

 needed to support Ship Spread / Detailed Ship Plans, found in the MCPIC Prepositioning 
Planning System (PPS) application.  4.D.10.d The MCMC shall arrange for Transportation Protective Services and ship downloaded weapons via bonded carrier to LOGCOM, or other Government designated facility, for maintenance IAW DTR 4500.9- R Part II.  When possible, direct shipment to 
LOGCOM upon download from MPF shall be arranged to minimize storage at BICmd Temporary Weapons Facility.  The MCMC shall notify the Government when same day shipment is not possible and storage is required.  The MCMC shall  conduct advance 
planning and liaison with the consignor, consignee, and Transportation Service Provider's (TSP) dispatcher for safe and secure transport of weapons, to include contacting destination activity to confirm the ability and intent to receive and secure the shipment.  DD Form 1907 (Signa ture and Tally Record) shall be prepared and retained for all 
Transportation Protective Services (TPS) shipments IAW DTR Part II.  The Report of Shipment (REPSHIP) shall be submitted for all weapon shipments.  
4.D.10.e.  MCPP -N Specific.   The USMC has prepositioned equipment in 
Norway for MCPP -N that contains all classes of supply to include crew served 
weapons.  Due to the sensitivity of weapons, this equipment requires U. S. personnel to account and store the equipment while it is prepositioned in Norway.  The MCMC shall perform the duties of Armory Custodian(s).  This requires a two-person integrity rule to be maintained during normal and extended working 
hours.  The MCMC per sonnel shall be responsible for all crew served weapons on 
the MMV420 account to include the following:  
 
• Administration and management, security, accountability and daily operations of the armory account to include the conduct of inventories  
• Storage/handling/disposition: safe storage and positive control of all weapons and conduct inspections to determine that proper physical security and control are provided 
• Transportation: proper physical security measures during transport of weapons  
• Establish, test, and maintain Emergency Action Plan (EAP)

 4.D.12. Requirements for Portable Fire Extinguishers associated with ME.   4.D.12.a NFPA Compliance. The MCMC shall adhere to the National Fire Protection 
Association (NFPA) 10- 18 standards for the inspection, maintenance, and testing of portable fire 
extinguishers.   4.D.12.b The MCMC shall centrally manage all MCPP portable fire extinguishers, 
developing standard operating procedures for tracking inspections, maintenance, and testing. This includes procedures for extinguisher tagging, marking, and labeling, as well as  annual, six-
year, and twelve -year inspections. Monthly and annual inspections are waived during the Afloat 
Phase for MPF.  
  4.D.12.c During MMC Production, the MCMC ensure all extinguishers are serviced, 
tested, and certified according to NFPA standards, including those resulting from SL -3 
configuration changes, fielding, or tailoring changes. Non- compliant extinguishers shall  be 
replaced with suitable alternatives.  
  4.D.12.d The MCMC shall document annual maintenance and hydrostatic testing for all 
portable fire extinguishers, maintaining official records in accordance with the FAR.  These records must be available to the Government upon request.  
  4.D.12.e All MCPP portable fire extinguishers utilized on MPS shall be certified by the 
manufacturer or serviced, inspected, and certified by an authorized service provider within 12 months of embarkation (E -Day).  Extinguishers at ashore locations must be serviced in line with 
NFPA timelines.  
  4.D.12.f Commercial service invoices shall identify the type of service (maintenance or 
hydrostatic test) work performed.  The service request and/or invoice shall reflect the organization that performed the work, description of service, the individual portable fire extinguisher’s serial number (when applicable), NSN, cost, qty, and date work was completed.  
  4.D.12.g Inspection Tags and Labels:

4.G T- AKE SUPPLY OPERATIONS    
 Supply and warehousing operations aboard the T-AKE ships include issuing consumable and expendable stocks, as well as secondary reparables to the MAGTF;  recovering unexpended ground ammunition;  replenishing stocks that were depleted as a result of issuing to the MAGTF; and conducting shipboard warehousing and storage operations to support the selective offload, replenishment, and restocking of consumables, expendables, and secondary reparables from/to the T -AKE ships.  
 4.G.1 The MCMC shall issue consumable/expendable stocks from the prepositioned sustainment blocks embarked aboard the T-AKE to the MAGTF during MPF exercises IAW MCO 4790.2, MCO P4400.151, UM 4000-125, the Government Exercise Support Letter for the specifi c 
exercise and Section C.7 Shipboard.  While Class II and VII (non -consumables) are issued to the 
MAGTF on a temporary loan basis in support of all MPF exercises and operations (as directed in Section 4.C.4.c), consumable/expendable stocks are issued to and consumed by the MAGTF during exercises that involve the use of T-AKE ships.  Consumables / expendables issued to the MAGTF (from the T -AKE) in support of exercises include Class I, III (P), IV, V (W), and IX 
(C).  Each of the classes of supply have unique requisitioning/request procedures by the MAGTF, resulting in specific document management and order fulfillment procedures required by the MCMC.  Class V (W) shall only be issued at the unit pack level unless otherwise directed by the Government.  Of not e, bulk fuel / Class III (B) is coordinated between the MAGTF and 
MSC, with no requirements levied upon the MCMC.   4.G.2 The MCMC shall provide Class IX Secondary Reparable supply support to the MAGTF during MPF exercises involving the use of T-AKE ships IAW the latest / current version of MCO 4790.2, MCO 4400.201, UM 4000-125, Government Exercise Support Letter for the specific exercise, and Section C.7 Shipboard. SECREP support shall be provided to the MAGTF, using the T- AKE sub -float accounts of MMFAQ61  and MMFAQ62 to issue serviceable 
SECREPs.  In nearly all situations, the T- AKE sub -float will issue the serviceable  SECREP with 
the MAGTF turning in the associated unserviceable SECREP carcass, which shall subsequently be evacuated to MMFAU1 for repair and return.  In other situations, although the MAGTF will initially be issued a serviceable SECREP from the T -AKE inve ntory to expedite the repair of the 
end item that the SECREP is associated to, the MAGTF will not turn in a carcass to the T -
AKE.  Instead, when time permits during the conduct of the exercise, the MAGTF will conduct a carcass exchange with its home statio n supporting RIP and obtain a serviceable replacement 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 154 of 425 
 
 SECREP from that RIPs OH inventory to replace the serviceable asset issued from the T -AKE, 
thereby avoiding any MAGTF reimbursement cost to the MPF program.  Lastly, there may be 
situations where the Government TAAT refuses to accept the MAGTFs unserviceab le carcass 
due to either its severely degraded condition or HAZMAT restrictions on the ship, resulting in an "issue (from the T -AKE) without a carcass turn -in (from the MAGTF)”.  In all situations, the 
Government TAAT will coordinate with the MAGTF and the  LOGCOM Coordinated SECREP 
Management (CSM) Office to determine MEF financial reimbursement  requirements, which is 
dependent upon whether an unserviceable carcass was turned in, which wholesale organization manages the SECREP, and whether or not the MAGTF can source a serviceable replacement SECREP during the conduct of the exercise.   
 4.G.3   Utilizing the Government -provided template for a "T -AKE Post -Exercise Consumption 
List” ( SOW Attachment C.4 -7), the MCMC shall track and report the issuing of consumables, 
expendables, and secondary reparables to the MAGTF from the prepositioned sustainment blocks embarked aboard the T -AKE during MPF exercises.  T -AKE Post -Exercise Consumption 
List ( CDRL 5045)  shall be accurate IAW physical materiel/consumables (type and quantity) that 
were issued to the MAGTF from the T -AKE, while considering any unconsumed or unexpended 
supplies that were eventually turned back in to the T -AKE prior to the exercise conclusion, 
providing total accounting for equipment and material issued to and  consumed by  the MAGTF.  
The T -AKE Post -Exercise Consumption List shall be a real -time tracker that is updated daily as 
supplies are issued, with the finalized list provided to the Governm ent (on- site TAAT / COTR) at 
the conclusion of the exercise or prior to the T -AKE departure from the exercise, whichever is 
earlier.  The final list shall be reconciled and agreed upon between the Government TAAT, MCMC, and MAGTF, as the Government will us e the list for funds reimbursement purposes 
from the MAGTF.  This list is separate and distinct from the "Post Exercise Discrepancy List”, 
which lists those CL IX(C) parts and SECREPs (associated with specific TAMCNs and serial numbers) that were NOT issue d from the T -AKE and require MCMC to open SRs after the 
exercise because repairs were not completed by the MAGTF during regeneration and prior to backload.  
 4.G.4 The MCMC shall recover unexpended Class V (W) stocks from the MAGTF during exercises where ammunition was issued from the T -AKE prepositioned sustainment blocks, to 
include updating OIS -PC to reflect current inventory status IAW the Government Exercise 
Support Letter for the specific exercise.  The Class V (W) physical inventory shall match the OIS-PC inventory records.  Any unexpended ammunition that remains in unit pack configuration 
and is properly sentenced can be returned to the T -AKE by the MAGT F.  Repackaging / 
sentencing of unexpended ammunition is a MAGTF requirement prior to turning back into the T -
AKE inventory.  
 
4.G.5 The MCMC shall replenish Class III(P), IV, and IX consumables of stocks issued to the MAGTF from the prepositioned sustainment blocks embarked aboard the T -AKE.  
Replenishment shall be to levels consistent with the quantities of the Detailed Ship Pla ns that the 
ships were loaded to during their respective MMC production cycle. MCMC replenishment of consumables is only required for Class III (P), IV, and IX consumables that were depleted during T-AKE exercises.  MCMC replenishment of these consumables shall be sourced from MMV200 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 155 of 425 
 
 as the first source of supply, with requisitions passed to wholesale level when not available from 
MMV200.  Government will coordinate Class I / MRE replenishment with DLA and Class V (W) with PM Ammo after respective exercises.  While the Government will coordinate 
replenishment of Class I and V, the MCMC will be responsible for rewarehousing of those stocks when pier side replenishment of the T-AKE occurs.   GCSS -MC requisitions shall be 
submitted within two business days following the issuance of the spe cific consumable stock to 
the MAGTF.  
 4.G.6  Upon issuing / exchanging SECREPs to / with the MAGTF during T- AKE exercises, the 
MCMC shall replenish the SECREP block embarked aboard the T- AKE ships with serviceable 
SECREPs.  In situations where the MAGTF turned in an unserviceable SECREP carcass in exchange for a serviceable SECREP from the T -AKE, replenishment of the T- AKE sub -float 
shall be conducted via carcass exchange with BICmd main float (MMFAU1).  If MMFAU1 has a servi ceable replacement on hand, it shall be sent to the T- AKE sub -float fo r replenishment 
purposes.  Conversely, if MMFAU1 does not have the serviceable stock immediately on hand, the main float shall coordinate the repair and return in order to bring the SECREP back to a serviceable state for the purpose of replenishing the T-A KE sub -float.  In situations where the 
exercise MAGTFs supporting RIP has a serviceable replacement SECREP on hand and time permits to ship the SECREP to the exercise location prior to MPF backload, the replenishment will come from the MAGTFs supporting RIP.  In cases where the MAGTF was issued a serviceable SECREP without a carcass turn -in, lateral support via LOGCOM shall be considered 
as an option.  If lateral support of enterprise wide excesses is not an option, the MCMC may be directed by the Government to purchase a replacement SECREP after the MAGTF reimburses the MPF Program for the serviceable SECREP it was issued from the T- AKE.  
 
4.G.7 The MCMC shall conduct warehousing operations aboard the T- AKE.  While the 
Government will develop and provide the embarkation plans that identifies ship holds and specific locations for square load assets (by class of supply with POLs, MREs, ammunition, medical supplies, batteries, repair parts, and secondary reparables segregated in designated locations), the MCMC shall create a warehousing scheme / locator deck that provides NSN- level 
visibility to facilitate rapid location verification and accessibility of embarked supplies for the purpose of selective offload in support of the MAGTF.  For example, the plans will not designate specific locations for smaller individual Class IX items (traditionally referred to as "bins”), and the MCMC shall be respo nsible for warehousing such items in the designated VSTF 
locations.   Warehousing operations shall also consider physical security requirements, shelf life management, refrigeration, and storage compatibility restrictions (ammunition).  The MCMC will not b e responsible for operating MHE aboard the ship, and instead, all MHE support shall be 
coordinated with the MSC staff aboard ship.  The T- AKE locator deck shall be accurate IAW 
physical on-hand inventory, and T-AKE supplies shall be maintained in a ready f or issue state.

 The MCMC shall manage ISO containers per DTR 4500.9- R Part VI and MCO 4690.1B while

JCM shall be the sole system for managing Marine Corps 20ft ISO container inventory, ensuring data accuracy per physical O/H inventory.  4.H.2 Container Accountability  The MCMC shall maintain accountability of Marine Corps -owned and leased ISO freight 
containers within MPS and ashore -based prepositioning programs per MCO 4690.1 and DTR

4.H.5 Container Maintenance  
 The MCMC shall execute a maintenance program covering repairs, corrosion control, blasting, and painting per MIL -STD -3037, DTR 4500.9- R Part VI, and ISO Standards Handbook.  
 
4.H.5.a All container markings (ISO serial numbers, owner’s codes, tare/net/max weights) shall comply with ISO Handbook, MIL -STD -3037, and DTR 4500.9- R Part VI.  
 4.H.5.b Code H containers (repair cost >65% of acquisition cost) shall be disposed via DLA- DS with Government approval. Shipping documentation shall be uploaded into the 
container management system within three business days of shipping.  
4.H.6 Quarterly Container Projection Report  
 The MCMC shall submit a Quarterly Container Projection Report ( CDRL 5047)  by the last 
business day of each quarter, detailing:  
• O/H quantity by type, condition code, and location 
• Washout rates and maintenance requirements  
• Container availability vs. requirements  
• Projected procurement needs  
 
4.H.7 Container Handling & Storage  
 Containers shall be handled per DTR 4500.9- R Part VI and MCO 4450.14 to prevent damage. 
Requirements include:  
• No direct ground contact (use railroad ties)  
• Proper MHE/CHE usage for movement  
• Secured doors/ramps before moving 
• Protection from elements for open containers  
• No crimped door gaskets in storage

 4.I.1 The MCMC shall conduct Transportation Management Shipping and receiving functions IAW DTR 4500.9-R Part II, MCO 4600.41B Marine Corps Distribution Manual, DoD 4500.32-R, MCO 4450.14, MILSTAMP, MIL- STD -129› Military Marking for Shipment and Storage and

Items include materiel with security requirements (CCI and weapons), AA & E, urgent delivery / high priority materiel, refrigeration / environmentally -controlled items, hazardous materiel, pilferable 
and sensitive items.  Notification of shipment arrival s hall be sent to responsible 
commodity the day of arrival with delivery / pick up / secure storage (where applicable) completed IAW Section 4.C.5.f "Inventory Management" requirements.

Container Seal Verification Report (CDRL 5048)  within one business day of 
completion of ammunition containers backload to MPF.  Minimum data elements on the report will include:  Container Serial Number, Seal Number associated with each container Serial Number.  The report shall be endorsed by the MCMC conducting the inspection and his/her supervisor.  Container Seal Verification Report shall be accurate IAW physical container serial number and associated seal number.

 4.I.1.b. MCPP- N Specific:  Foreign military equipment or materiel being transported 
across the Norwegian border is subject to prior approval from NJHQ. This includes equipment and materiel coming in by road, rail, air or sea.  The MCMC shall provide MCPP -N Weekly Equipment Clearance report (CDRL 5049) ( SOW Attachment C.4-11)

(ii) The MCMC shall ship equipment to meet TRANSCOM requirements, Norwegian Customs, and Agricultural regulations.  Equipment shipped meets USTRANSCOM, Norwegian Customs, and Agricultural regulations.  All Shipping documentation and required signatures shall be accurate.  Shipping documentation shall be but is not limited to DD Form 1149, DD Form 1348-1, freight bill and applicable shipping manifests. 
 4.I.1.c. MCPP-PHIL Specific: The Republic of the Philippines (Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA)) treats MCPP -PHIL’s prepositioned equipment shipments as a primarily 
commercial activity governed by the regulations of the 2022 Foreign Investment Act, its accompanying Negative List, and relevant regulations of the Subic Bay Management Authority (SBMA).  The MCMC shall attain status as a licensed commercial importer in the Philippines, attain a Certificate of Registration (CR) and a Certificate of Registratio n 
and Tax Exemption (CRTE).  MCMC shall be responsible for attaining import/export clearances of MCPP -PHIL equipment entering into SBMA via commercial/contracted 
shipping through a coordinated End User Certificate and Statement of Undertaking with SBMA and  any necessary local customs brokers or support providers.  Equipment 
imported into Subic is tax -free because Subic is a tax -free special economic zone.  In the 
case that the USMC wants to move the equipment from Subic as part of a bilateral military exerc ise, for Humanitarian Assistance/Disaster Response, or further storage at an 
EDCA site, then BICmd will coordinate with the U.S. Embassy and attain the necessary certificate of tax -exemption under the Visiting Forces Agreement (VFA) to “import” the 
equipme nt into the Philippines from the SBMA free trade zone.  MCMC shall facilitate 
government coordination by preparing any required shipment documentation such as DD-1149/1750, bills of lading, packing lists etc. 
 If MCPP -PHIL equipment, supplies arrive 
on a U .S. military vessel, BICmd will coordinate with the US Embassy to attain 
import/export clearances, which the MCMC shall facilitate by providing required documentation.

4.J  FISCAL REQUIREMENTS.  
 4.J.1  This subsection outlines the responsibilities related to the effective management and accurate accounting of funds utilized through Department of Defense (DoD) supply systems and other government -funded procurement channels. A high degree of precisi on is essential to 
ensure that requisitions are processed in an efficient, economical manner. By maintaining rigorous standards, the Marine Corps upholds public trust and reinforces confidence in its ability to responsibly manage resources.  
 
M6700425R0007

 4.J.1.a  Scope. The Marine Corps Maintenance Coordinator (MCMC) is tasked with the 
comprehensive management and precise accounting of funds expended to support maintenance cycle operations. This includes costs associated with repair parts, shelf -life 
replacements, SL -3 unit responsibility items, shipboard operations, and additional 
logistics support efforts. The MCMC holds responsibility for the complete lifecycle ("cradle to grave") financial oversight of all supply- related actions, ensuring adherence to 
published Fiscal Year -end Closeout guidelines and delivering required status reports. 
 4.J.1.b  All references to the Government within Section 4.H specifically pertain to the BICmd Resource Management Office (RMO) as the primary government entity.  4.J.1.c  The MCMC is responsible for delivering a timely and accurate electronic Contract Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF) and Status Report (CCSR) (CDRL 5057) for each Task Order (TO) in accordance with Section G-6. The CCSR must be submitted on a monthly basis, or upon request by the Government, concurrently with invoice submissions, starting 30 days after the contract award date.

 4.J.2   Accounting  
4.J.2.a  Funding Authorizations.  MCMC shall:  
 
(i)  Ensure that funds are expended in accordance with the authorized amounts allocated and recorded in the Supported Activities Information File (SAIF) ledger within GCSS -MC. 
 (ii)  Submit a request to increase authorized amounts as necessary, ensuring it is 
completed prior to anticipated spending requirements.

4.J.2.b  Material Returns Program (MRP) Management.   MCMC shall:  
 
(i)  Prepare and provide a comprehensive report of all MRP transactions to the 
Government. This memorandum report should include a detailed record of all 
credit billings (FD2/1) received during the fiscal year. It must be maintained on 
the shared drive for  the Government’s review as needed. The memorandum 
records shall be updated daily to reflect new billing transactions as they appear in DAI.  
 (ii) Send a follow -up regarding the credit due if the credit billing has not been 
received within 60 calendar days from the date the FTZ with creditable status was processed.  
 
4.J.3  Supply Fiscal Reconciliations  
 
4.J.3.a   Fiscal/Supply Reconciliations.   MCMC shall ensure that appropriate supply 
transactions are balanced with the accounting system records. This process shall include the following tasks:  
 
(i)  Perform reconciliation between official accounting reports and memorandum records following each accounting cycle update. It is important to note that DAI cycle updates are processed daily, except on Sundays. Updates processed on business days shall be reconciled on the same day. Saturday updates will be addressed on the first business day of the subsequent week.  
 (ii)  Ensure that all GCSS -MC requisitions and offline system requisitions are 
accurately obligated and posted in DAI. Offline requisitions, including Prime Vendor purchases that are not obligated through a mechanized feeder system, must be manually entere d into DAI after proper validation within the supply 
system. Additionally, ensure that status and receipt documents from GCSS -MC 
align with the corresponding records in DAI.  
 (iii)  Correct or request correction of all supply records in GCSS -MC and 
accounting records in  DAI found to be in error either by manual research or by usage of an automated system.  
 (iv)  Conduct daily reconciliations of the   DNR Unmatched Report   and update 
error records as required on a daily basis. All errors should be corrected on the same day it appears on the error report. If MCMC is unable to correct the error immediately, MCMC  shall immediately notify Government.  
 (v)  Monitor GCSS -MC DASF reports daily to identify cancellations and ensure 
timely processing of corrective actions.

 (vi)  Immediately report and identify accounting system issues to the Government 
upon discovery, ensuring all necessary supporting documentation is provided.  (vii)  Ensure the storage and maintenance of all source documents and accounting 
reports required for audits for a period of 10 years. This includes receipt documentation for both supply system and offline system requisitions. 
 
4.J.3.c  Unliquidated Orders (ULO) Reviews.   MCMC shall:  
 
(i)  Provide a monthly reconciliation discrepancy report based on internal ULO, GCSS -MC DASF, and VLIPS validations. 
 (ii)  Conduct monthly validations for all current fiscal year ULOs and ULOs from 
the five preceding fiscal years.  
 (iii)  Submit a monthly ULO validation report (CDRL 5052) to the Government 
within 15 calendar days after the last day of the month for current fiscal year ULOs. The ULO validation report for August shall follow End of Year Closeout Guidance.  
 (iv)  Take immediate corrective actions in DAI to adjust or cancel ULOs found to be invalid.  (v)  Review all ULOs with processed receipts. For receipts older than 30 days, request billing actions in writing (via email or letter) from the appropriate Source of Supply (SOS).   (vi)  Review all liquidations with no associated expenses. Provide document numbers to the appropriate supply personnel to ensure receipts are processed within 3 –4 days of material delivery. 
 (vii)  Act on documents requested by the Government in support of BEV reviews 
for ULOs from current and prior years, in accordance with guidance from Headquarters Marine Corps (HQMC) and Marine Corps Logistics Command (MCLC). These reviews, similar to ULO validations, are typically required monthly.  (viii)   Take appropriate action on documents provided by the Government in 
support of BEV reviews for ULOs from current and prior years, in compliance with guidance from Headquarters Marine Corps (HQMC) and Marine Corps Logistics Command (MCLC). These revi ews, similar to ULO validations, are 
typically conducted on a monthly basis. Ensure submission of BEV reviews within 15 calendar days following January 31, May 31, and September 30. (Remove this)

 4.J.4  Fiscal Closeout Guidance.  MCMC shall adhere to the annual Fiscal Year (FY) closeout 
guidance provided by the Contracting Officer. 
  
4.J.5  Supply Fiscal Reports 
 
4.J.5.a  Status Report.  MCMC shall provide the Government with a weekly Status of 
Funds report (CDRL 5055), adhering to government-approved accounting procedures. 
The report shall include, at a minimum, the following information: 
 
• Posted Authorizations 
• Posted Obligations 
• Posted Available Balance 
• Pending Corrections 
• Revised Available Balance  
• Allocations Received  
• MRP Credits Received  
 
4.J.5.b  Reimbursable Report.  MCMC shall  provide a monthly Reimbursable Report 
(CDRL 5056): 
 
(i)  Submit a monthly status report and daily transaction listing detailing general account charges for all non -MPF funding sources, including Navy, MCPP- N, 
MCPP -PHIL, and Other Logistics Support (OLS). These reports shall be 
submitted electronically to BICmd RMO, the Navy Financial Management Analyst, the MCPP-N Supply Analyst, the MCPP-PHIL Supply Analyst, and the designated OLS analyst.  (ii)  Ensure the submission of the monthly status report, detailing both monthly 
and cumulative general account charges by customers, no later than the 5th of each month.  
4.J.6 Fiscal Requirements for Manpower  
4.J.6.a   Ensure a minimum of five personnel are dedicated to supporting fiscal requirements, filling key roles such as team lead, procurement officer, P2P receipts manager, and other necessary positions to ensure the timely completion of all functions outlined in Section 4.J. 
 4.K EXEMPTIONS AND WAIVERS  
 
4.K.1 Although the MCMC shall manage the day- to-day supply functions and operations 
associated with Supply Officer responsibilities, as outlined in Marine Corps Orders, the authority to approve adjustments to accountable property records rests solely with the Government, regardless of dollar value.  The MCMCs roles and responsibilities include identification of recommended property record adjustments (via voucher requests with appropriate causative

of the resulting Money Value Gain Loss (MVGL) to the Government for review and signature.   
 4.K.2  The MCMC shall abide by all Department of Defense, Navy and Marine Corps small arms orders, directives, policies, and procedures unless otherwise directed by the Government.  While the MCMC is responsible for small arms accountability and associated  transaction 
processing using the approved APSR, the MCMC shall not conduct small arms reporting or annual Crane verification with Naval Surface Warfare Center Crane Division (NSWC Crane).   Rather, the Government will conduct small arms reporting with NSW C Crane, to 
include completing requirements associated with the annual Crane verification.    
 MCPP -N Specific:  The MCMC shall take the following actions to report assets, establish 
local accountability, and handle MCPP -N small arms.  The MCMC shall upload all 
required LST reports applicable to serialized small arms to the Government provided SharePoint site.  Two -person integrity is required in Norway for armory access and 
accountability during NDLO MEB business hours (0730- 1530 local).  
 4.K.3  Minimum shelf life requirements for prepositioning of materiel with shelf life codes is contradictory amongst several orders and policy.  As it relates to minimum duration of shelf life 
remaining on materiel at the time of embarkation aboard MPF, the MCMC shall follow the guidance outlined in MCO 4000.58, which supersedes MCO 4140.5 and MCO 3000.17 in this specific area.  
 4.K.4  The MCMC shall inventory SL -3 annually during PMCS and place SL -3 equipment in 
accordance with stowage configurations as identified in technical publications as applicable (SL -
3 can be stored in boxes and labeled with the equipment serial number.  O nce inventoried, SL -3 
shall be stored securely with locks and/or authorized banding material.)  
  
SECTION C.5 –  MEDICAL  
 
This Section sets forth the requirements and responsibilities of the MCMC for the management and support services associated with Class VIII medical material in support of Maritime Prepositioning Force (MPF), Marine Corps Prepositioning Program -Norway (MCP P-N), MCPP -
PHIL, and future ashore sites.  The MCMC shall p rovide quality control oversight to the Class 
VIII medical consumables and equipment of MCPP contract as it relates to the three principals of contract performance; management effectiveness, cost control, and quality.   Medical Material Management at all locations governed by the contract shall be performed in support of the Marine Corps Prepositioning Program (MCPP) in accordance with Government -
approved production schedules.  Products shall conform to the specifications  described within 
this section.  Quality assurance measurements will be governed by the performance requirements

integrity is maintained.  For MPF, the MCMC shall ensure a 36 -month minimum expiration date

receiving discrepancies completing the SF -361 Transportation Discrepancy Report.

and DMLSS. IAW NAVMC 4000.2A section 2.1, MCMC shall utilize DMLSS for line -item 
accountability and GCSS -MC for TAMCN accountability.

5.D The MCMC shall perform inventory accountability.   
 
5.D.1 The MCMC shall conduct an annual 100 percent wall -to-wall inventory for all MPF assets 
upon download IAW MCO 4400.201.  The MCMC shall deliver the Inventory Findings Report and Memorandum.  MCPP -N, MCPP -PHIL, and future ashore sites medical inventori es will be 
planned with consideration of on- island production schedules.  
 
5.D.1.a MCMC shall p rovide an Inventory Adjustment Report to the Government within 
7 calendar days of inventory.  
(i) The MCMC shall provide an inventory voucher report for any inventory adjustment report discrepancies within 7 calendar days of inventory. Attachment C.5.D.1.a.i provides an example of the inventory voucher report in DMLSS. 
 5.D.2 The MCMC shall complete and submit a DD Form 200 IAW MCO 4400.201 volume 17 chapter 2 if an item cannot be located.  The DD Form 200 shall be submitted within 30 calendar  
days of identifying an item is missing.  
 5.D.3 The MCMC shall retain a copy of all supply documents.  The MCMC shall retain all supply documents IAW the FAR and MCO 4400.201 (Vol. 1).  
 5.D.4 The MCMC shall manage short shelf -life material IAW DoD 4140.27- M (Vol. 1).  
 5.D.5 The MCMC shall execute recall procedures IAW NAVMC 4000.2A (section 9.2). The MCMC shall provide a Suspensions and Recall Items Notification.  Information is available via United States Army Medical Material Agency (USAMMA) and Medical Material Quality Control (MMQC).  The MCMC’s written response is required NLT 7 calendar days of notification.  The MCMC shall retain associated quality deficiency reports, recalls, and 
notifications for three years.  
 
5.E The MCMC shall perform maintenance on all Authorized Medical Allowance List (AMAL) and Authorized Dental Allowance List (ADAL) equipment IAW NAVMED P -5132.  MCPP -N, 
MCPP -PHIL, and future ashore sites medical maintenance will be planned with consideration of 
on-island production schedules. 
 5.E.1 The MCMC shall perform preventative equipment maintenance IAW NAVMED P -5132 
and applicable technical manuals. If the manufacturer or DMLSS literature conflict with the NAVMED P -5132, the manufacturer or DMLSS literature take precedence.    
 5.E.2  The MCMC shall maintain a scheduled maintenance program for medical equipment IAW  NAVMED P -5132 (article 15- 2 section 6a). Maintenance schedule dates for all equipment shall 
be entered into DMLSS.  5.E.3  The MCMC shall assign all pieces of medical equipment to an AMAL in DMLSS block IAW NAVMED P -5132 (Article 15- 3 section 4c(1)).

5.E.2  The MCMC shall place a copy of the equipment manual with the equipment IAW NAVMED P -5132.  
 5.E.3  The MCMC shall utilize DMLSS as the sole APSR to track all maintenance performed on Class VIII items, document maintenance and repair efforts, and maintain repair parts accountability in accordance with NAVMED P -5132 (Article 15- 2, Section 7).  
 
5.E.3.a  Actions performed on any equipment shall be documented in the proper location of DMLSS IAW NAVMED P -5132 (article 15 -4 section 5).  
 5.E.3.b  All operational and preventative maintenance checks must be annotated in DMLSS within 7 calendar days as per NAVMED P -5132. If equipment fails operational tests, the MCMC 
shall submit a Quality Deficiency Report (QDR) to the Defense Logistics Agency and create a DMLSS work order for non -operational equipment. 
 
5.E.4 The MCMC shall place the original DMLSS active report with the equipment IAW NAVMED P -5132.  
 5.E.5 The MCMC shall notify the Government and annotate in DMLSS any recalled equipment within 7 calendar days IAW NAVMED P -5132. 
 5.E.6 The MCMC shall maintain a technical library for equipment IAW NAVMED P -5132 
(Article 15 -4, section 12).  At a minimum one operator’s manual, one service manual, and one 
parts/list manual for each model type of equipment maintained at BICmd.  
 5.E.7 The MCMC shall complete equipment receipt and inspection procedures IAW NAVMED P-5132 Article 8- 5.Acceptance inspections shall be executed and documented into DMLSS 
within  14 calendar  days upon receipt. If new equipment fails the acceptance inspection, the 
MCMC shall complete a SF -368 IAW NAVMED P -5132 Article 7- 4 and notify the Government 
within 7 calendar  days.  
 5.E.8  T he MCMC shall provide the Government with a Surplus Equipment Report, detailing 
any equipment quantities greater than the PO, within the first 7 calendar days of the month. 
 
5.F The MCMC shall produce AMAL/ADAL blocks for embarkation. 
 
5.F.1 The MCMC shall assemble all AMAL/ADAL blocks IAWNAVMC 2907 MPF PO, the government approved production schedule and the MLO MCRW AMAL packing list. Do not embark NSN 6505 material except for intravenous solutions and medical gasses IAW NAVMC 4000.2A section 6.2.. Written Government approval shall be obtained prior to embarking narcotics, controlled medical items, precious metal indicator or refrigerated material.  
 5.F.2 The MCMC shall place late attainment items in the block IAW the approved production schedule and NAVMC 2907.

5.F.3 The MCMC shall pack hazardous material IAW the government approved production schedule and ship spread loads found in the MCPIC Prepositioning Planning System (PPS) application.  A Hazardous Material List shall be part of "Post- Production Documents."  
 5.F.4 The MCMC shall properly mark all AMAL/ADAL Environmental Container System (ECS) IAW MILSTD -2073- 1E and MMS NO. 1A. 
 5.G The MCMC shall perform all disposition services.

5132.  5.I.  MCPP -N Specific.  All MCPP -N AMALS will be maintained, stored, and replenished in

Annually the MCMC shall conduct replenishment, maintenance, and calibration of AMALS in Norway.  Every third year, the MCMC shall ensure all AMALS are refreshed 100% and maintenance complete and ready for issue.  
 5.G  MCPP -PHIL, and future ashore sites.  All MCPP -PHIL and future ashore sites’ AMALS 
will be maintained, stored, and replenished at the respective geographic location.  Annually, the MCMC shall identify all expired material and equipment requiring mainte nance or calibration.  
All maintenance, calibration, and replenishment shall occur at the respective geographic location.  Annually, the MCMC shall conduct replenishment, maintenance, and calibration of AMALS at the respective geographic location.  Every t hird year, the MCMC shall ensure all AMALS are 
100% complete and ready for issue.

Navy and Marine Corps Orders and directives, Department of Defense guidance and directives, Federal, State and Local laws and regulations , industry standards, and 
other technical documentation.    Navy products and services at all locations governed by this contract shall be performed in support of the MCPP IAW approved production and delivery schedules. Quality Control measurements will be governed by the performance requirements and standards asso ciated with 
this section and Section C.2.D. Requirements and clarifications identified herein are those specific to Navy equipment, services and product delivery in support of the MCPP II contract. Continuous Process Improvement (CPI) and safety should be intertwined in all facets supporting the Navy product. MCMC management should strive to make prudent decisions regarding expenditures to be reimbursed by the Government and Government Supply System acquisitions. All changes from written standards will be r eviewed on a case- by-case basis and shall have 
approval from the Contracting Officer prior to deviations.    Navy "Over and Above" tasks require prior authorization IAW DFARS 252.217- 7028.   
  Navy supply and maintenance administration shall be documented in the Naval Tactical Command Support System (NTCSS), Organizational Maintenance Management System -Next 
Generation (OMMS -NG) and Relational Supply (RSupply) modules, or their replacements.  
  
The Lighterage Maintenance Model ( SOW Attachment C.1 -3, Phase IV) represents Watercraft 
maintenance steps that take place over an ~18 -month process that includes Preventive 
Maintenance (PM) and Corrective Maintenance (CM). Corrective Maintenance includes Organizational, Intermediate and Depot Level Maintenance. Navy Watercraft maintenance is an evolution that occurs in parallel to Marine Corps operations; but, has its own milestones that significantly precedes the Marine Corps MMC model. That is, the Waterc raft maintenance 
process starts well in advance of the Marine Corps maintenance cycle; but ends with watercraft backload onto specific ships along with the Marine Corps equipment. For short periods of time, there may be several ship sets of management stoc k watercraft at home station and there may be 
two or more desegregated ship sets in differing stages of the Lighterage Maintenance Model. This maintenance model is supported by rotational assets that allow the longer duration maintenance availabilities.     The Lighterage Maintenance Model has four major phases. The list of tasks within Tables 1 through 3 outlined in SOW Attachment C.1- 3 is a general overview and not meant to be a 
comprehensive list. Detailed deliverables are in this Section.  
  Phase Alpha is the Afloat Phase with watercraft embarked aboard Maritime Prepositioning Ships (MPS) and is considered the beginning of the Maintenance Model. This Phase is ~36- 42 months 
IAW the MMC schedule. Preventive and Corrective Maintenance for afloat  watercraft is 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 173 of 425 
 
 accomplished in a separate contract referred to as Navy Watercraft Afloat Maintenance Services 
(NWAMS) for the Afloat Maintenance Activity (AMA). However, SOW Section 6.G details specific requirements that provide maintenance administration, shipping and s upply support for 
the AMA.   
  Phase Bravo starts the ashore maintenance cycle with offload from ship, Phase Alpha closeout, custody transfer, pier side inspections, pier side operational tests, underway condition assessments and eventual docking/cleaning for further comprehensive asses sments/surveys. This 
Phase is 45 days in duration IAW the Lighterage Maintenance Milestone and may require emergent repair consisting of PM or CM, depending on its arrival condition. Emergent, unplanned watercraft regeneration may be necessary in order to (IOT) conduct underway operational tests of all systems by the Government appointed operators during a Pre MMC -
Assessment Trial (Pre -MAT). The requirements for Phase Bravo are depicted in SOW Section 
6.F. Accurate and thorough condition assessments during this phase are critical for establishing follow on work packages for Phase Charlie that will be detailed during the Work Definition Conference (WDC) process. Phase Bravo continues after testing with watercraft transport to the Watercraft Maintenance Center  (WMC), where watercraft are dry -docked, cleaned and surveyed.  
Phase Bravo is typically a joint effort between the Repair Support Team (RST), the Operations Support Team (OST), Government Operators and NAV -P.  
  Phase Bravo may also involve a Materiel Inspection Team designated by the President, Inspection and Survey (INSURV).     Phase Charlie is the second ashore phase of the model and is when intermediate and depot level Corrective Maintenance is scheduled and accomplished. This phase is the longest, with a 
duration of ~12 months or more, depending on the MMC Ship schedule and squadron spread load for watercraft. Due to Marine Corps Support Facility -  Blount Island (MCSF -BI) facilities 
limitations, much of the work identified during the WDC process may be outsourced/subcontracted to local ship repair facilities by MCMC Navy Operat ions via MCMC 
processes for Planning & Estimating, and Source Selection. Maintenance scope and funding availability is determined and approved by the Government. Intermediate maintenance and some Depot level maintenance may be performed at MCSF -BI, dependi ng on size, scope and MCMC 
vendor ability to meet MCSF -BI environmental and safety requirements. Skillsets to perform 
such work would consist of welder (gas, arc, MIG, TIG), machinist, shipfitter, marine mechanic, marine electrician, plumber, hydraulics, H VAC, rigger, and possibly others. MCSF -BI Navy 
Point shall be maintained as an organized maintenance and staging area and not appear as a Shipyard. All organizational, intermediate or depot level work performed at MCSF -BI shall be 
performed in an organized, inspection ready manner projecting a military appearance with work areas clear of debris, tools and supplies put away each day. Maintenance support gear shall be stored in adjacent storage containers and other support gear stored neatly under or near the  
affected craft at the end of each workday.  
  Due to the length, environmental conditions, and complexity of Phase Charlie, the watercraft monitoring (Care of Supplies in Storage (COSIS)) requirements outlined in Section C.6.F are essential to prevent progressive damage. During Phase Charlie, individual watercraft may 
M6700425R0007  
DRAFT  RFP 
 
Page 174 of 425 
 
 receive maintenance at several different local facilities (as outsourced by the MCMC) based on 
the Government approved work packages. Phase Charlie typically has ~52 different watercraft in various stages of maintenance, all on different schedules. Managem ent of each Phase Charlie 
maintenance availability, complete with quality control oversight and the schedule complexities is critical. Additionally, Phase Charlie may also include maintenance availability/periods for System Command (SYSCOM) sponsored requi rements, as well as other Government managed 
contracted maintenance efforts external to the MCPP II contract. Technical Refresh (TR) of watercraft is also conducted during Phase Charlie. Schedule creep should be avoided. Phase Charlie bleeding over into Phase Delta confounds the process and puts Phase D and the backload schedule at risk.  
  Phase Delta contains five unique stages that methodically move watercraft through the Navy MMC production cycle toward a specific ship’s backload at E+5. This Phase moves a designated ship set of watercraft through multiple stages of organizational level m aintenance outlined in the 
Maintenance Requirements Package (MRP) ( SOW Attachment C.6 -024) that prepare watercraft 
for MPS embarkation that is aligned with the USMC MMC process and MMC schedule. Phase Delta is primarily PM, but it may include O -level CM an d/or specific intermediate level 
maintenance within the MCMC capabilities/skillsets. This phase includes Control System configuration performed by the SYSCOM on- site support team. Phase Delta ends with 
operational tests, Government acceptance of the waterc raft and eventual MPS backload.   
  Finally, it is important to understand that all three ashore phases, Bravo, Charlie and Delta, are concurrently producing multiple ship sets of watercraft on different schedules. Projected notional quantities of watercraft for each maintenance phase is det ailed in SOW Attachment C.1 -9.   
  
Navy Watercraft (Lighterage) may be transported and transferred to external, independent contractors during Phase Alpha, Bravo, Charlie and Delta periods. Over and above maintenance requirements and external vendors will be identified by the Government. In addition, over and above maintenance requirements may be identified by SYSCOM by way of Fleet Service Bulletins (FSBs), Safety of Use Messages (SOUMs), Engineering Change Notices (ECNs), and System Alterations (SYSALTs) which may be tasked to the MCMC.   MCPP II applies to the majority of the NSE equipment. However, the Sealift portfolio is divided up and maintained by several different external contracts as detailed in the maintenance model above, physical location, custodial assignments and levels of mai ntenance, whether 
Organizational, Intermediate and/or Depot.       
  Section C.6 supersedes all other sections of this Statement of Work (SOW) concerning Navy requirements. Contact the Navy Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) for further clarification, as needed.

The Navy element shall leverage USMC maintenance/production services for Navy ground 
tactical gear/Civil Engineer Support Equipment (CESE). Additionally, the MCMC shall provide the following services: welding/fabrication services, wash rack services, batte ry storage services, 
trucking services, scale house functions, Preservation, Packaging and Packing (PP&P), carpentry, machinist, shipping services, calibration services, communication/green gear services, tug services/barrier open/closing services, Corrosi on Repair Facility (CRF) services, Rough 
Terrain Forklift (RTFL), Container Handling/Rough Terrain Container Handler (RTCH) and certification services, MOGAS, Diesel supply, and disposal services.   
  
6.A.1.a  The MCMC shall establish a dedicated Navy Program Manager as Key 
personnel. The Navy Program Manager will be directly supported by the following 
functionalities: Purchasing, Financial, 3M Data (Navy Maintenance and Material 
Management), Plans/Proj ects, Quality Control, and Subcontract Management.   Detailed 
below are the direct report Managers and their respective sections supporting the Navy effort.  
  6.A.1.b  The MCMC shall establish and maintain a dedicated Operations Support Team 
(OST). The OST supports and performs operational tests of lighterage systems, 
troubleshooting, watercraft movement (portage), watercraft monitoring/COSIS, and 
associated sup port functions such as lifting, rigging, staging, docking/undocking, cross -
decking, drydocking, load testing, berthing, fendering, mooring, floating piers and docks, 
fueling/defueling, craft dewatering. OST supports RST during Phase Bravo and Phase Charlie  evolutions. Crane Operations (300 Ton Crawler Crane), Weight/Material 
Handling Equipment (Heavy- Lift Transporters (HLT), Mobile Boat Hoists (MBH), 
forklifts, man -lift, organic equipment, Rough Terrain Container Handler (RTCH)), and 
movement of other non- watercraft assets. The OST may be required to work irregular 
hours (Sat, Sun, shiftwork) in support of operational tests and/or ship offload/backload events. Flex hours are authorized with prior Government approval. The OST Manager is 
designated Key Personnel.  
  
6.A.1.c  The MCMC shall establish and maintain a Navy Watercraft Maintenance Center 
(WMC) organization that maintains marine technicians with occupational skill sets to accomplish the Maintenance Requirements Package (MRP) ( SOW Attachment C.6 -024) 
and O -Level troubleshooting and Corrective Maintenance. Occupational skill sets shall 
be marine technician in type and shall include at a minimum: Electrical, Electronics,

(LARC) and the LARC Maintenance Facility (LMF) is considered an element of the WMC. LMF occupational skill sets shall be marine in type and shall include at a minimum: Supervisory, Electrical, Mechanical and Material Handler. The WMC Manager is design ated Key personnel.  
  6.A.1.d  The MCMC shall establish and maintain a dedicated Navy Supply Center (NSC) organization that maintains occupational skill sets to accomplish supply chain, warehousing, and Capability Set (CAPSET) container production. Occupational skill sets shall  include at a minimum: Supervisory, Logistics Specialist, Materiel Specialist,

The NSC Manager is Key personnel.    6.A.1.e  The MCMC shall establish and maintain a dedicated Controls System Support (CSS) organization that maintains occupational skill sets to accomplish Machinery Control System Technical Refresh, FSB and SYSALT installation, and other electronic or electrical support. Occupational skill sets shall be marine technician in type and shall include at a minimum: Supervisory, Electrical, Electronic and Mechanical. These marine technicians shall be in addition to those included in OST and WMC. The CSS Manager i s 
Key personnel.  
  
6.A.1.f  The MCMC shall establish and maintain a dedicated Repair Support Team (RST) that manages/oversees subcontract repair work on designated Navy lighterage and 
provides quality control oversight of subcontracted repairs and maintenance. The Repair Officer/Manager shall be supported by the following skill sets/specialists: Ship fitting, Corrosion Control, Hull Surveying, and Planning/Estimating, each with their respective certifications.  The RST may be required to work irregular hours (Sat, Sun, shiftw ork) in 
support of aligning Quality Control requirements to shipyard/repair production. Flex hours are authorized. The Repair Manager is Key personnel.  
  The RST shall evaluate pricing submissions received from their subcontractors and negotiate for best value prior to submitting to Government for approval. This process shall be in place for new work, growth work, and Condition Found Reports (CFRs) that carry an estimate for recommended work items associated with current effort.  
  
6.A.1.g  The MCMC shall establish and maintain a dedicated Maintenance Integration Manager (MIM) that manages integration between the maintenance sections of WMC, CSS and RST to produce watercraft CAPSETs. The MIM is a direct report to the Navy PM and is c onsidered Key personnel.  
 6.A.1.h The MCMC shall establish and maintain a dedicated Navy Quality Control Manager (QCM) that manages Quality Control processes for Navy Operations, including

 6.A.3.d   The MCMC shall notify the appropriate Responsible Officer (RO), Accountable Property Officer (APO), Assistant Contracting Officer’s Representative (ACOR) or Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) of any missing, lost, stolen, broken, damaged or otherwise unusable GFE within 3 business days (CDRL 6003)  via email.
